Original_Search_Title: Habila Seeks Encouragement for Upcoming Writers
Headline: Nigeria: Habila Seeks Encouragement for Upcoming Writers
Body_Text:
Celebrated writer, Helon Habila, was recently a guest of the Advancement Office of the University of Jos to deliver a Distinguished Alumni Lecture as part of his tour of Nigeria. He spoke to Sunday Vanguard on the occasion.
HOW does it feel coming back to your former university after some years?

Original_Search_Title: Esther Lalam, a Teacher in Northern Uganda - an Xmas Feast And Reunion
Headline: Uganda: Esther Lalam, a Teacher in Northern Uganda - an Xmas Feast And Reunion
Body_Text:
Kitgum — This is part of a special IRIN series: Uganda Diaries, in which a selection of ordinary people in northern Uganda talk about their lives in their own words. The "diaries" were gathered over several interviews in Uganda starting from July 2007.
December 14

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Warns Mengo, CBS On Land
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Warns Mengo, CBS On Land
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni yesterday addressed a press conference on the question of land. Alfred Wasike and Raymond Baguma transcribed the President's speech
I want to comment on the statements that have been made on some of the radios especially CBS regarding land. I would like to make four points on that issue.

Original_Search_Title: Standing Up to Bullies
Headline: Uganda: Standing Up to Bullies
Body_Text:
Don't you just hate those times when you are stuck at the park and the number of people waiting at your stage could fill 10 kamunyes yet only one comes along every 10 minutes?
Here I am stranded and have been standing here since 6p.m.

Original_Search_Title: Pine Trees Vital, Says VP Bukenya
Headline: Uganda: Pine Trees Vital, Says VP Bukenya
Body_Text:
Kampala — PEOPLE with small pieces of land in rural areas should engage in tree planting that will bring them both income and firewood, the vice president, Gilbert Bukenya, has said.
According to a statement issued by the VP's Press Unit yesterday, Bukenya, who was speaking at his tree-garden in Kakiri, said the misconception that tree planting is a reserve for the rich who own bigger chunks of land must be discarded.

Original_Search_Title: Sanyu Babies Home Gets Donation
Headline: Uganda: Sanyu Babies Home Gets Donation
Body_Text:
Kampala — CHRISTMAS came early for the children of Sanyu Babies Home in Kampala when they received donations from the Indian community under the Shree Kutch Satsang Swaminarayan Temple.
Handing over the items on Tuesday, Nitin Vekariya, the community chairman, flanked by other members of the temple, said the donations worth over sh3 million were also made to five different homes for the disabled people and orphanages in Kampala and Jinja.

Original_Search_Title: 'The EU Is a Bandit in Trade Negotiations'
Headline: Ghana: 'The EU Is a Bandit in Trade Negotiations'
Body_Text:
Accra — A lively debate has been taking place in Ghana for some time now over the likely effects that an economic partnership agreement (EPA) with the European Union would have on the country. This has been thanks in no small part to the work of Tetteh Hormeku, one of Africa's most vocal campaigners on trade issues.
The European Commission, the EU's executive arm, is adamant that nearly 80 African, Caribbean and Pacific (ACP) countries must sign interim EPAs - relating to trade in goods - by the end of this year, with talks on a range of other issues, such as services liberalisation and investment rules continuing into 2008.

Original_Search_Title: Different Kind of Battle
Headline: Sierra Leone: Different Kind of Battle
Body_Text:
Freetown — Sierra Leone's economy has, over the years, relied heavily on the mining sector in general and diamonds in particular. However, between 1991 and 2000 the country was comprehensively destroyed in a brutal civil war that engulfed the West African state.
Seven years after the war was declared over, the country is still struggling to reactivate economic activities from yesteryear -- despite praise from the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund for its economic progress during the post-conflict transition.

Original_Search_Title: Group Debunks Myth of Urban Attraction By Sticking to Land
Headline: Kenya: Group Debunks Myth of Urban Attraction By Sticking to Land
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Simon Ouma is one of the few Kenyans who detest rural-urban migration by those in search of white-collar jobs.
The 28-year-old Siaya farmer can comfortably take care of his family, thanks to a project he and others started two years ago.

Original_Search_Title: Famine to Hit 11 Districts
Headline: Uganda: Famine to Hit 11 Districts
Body_Text:
Kampala — ELEVEN districts in northern and north-eastern Uganda face imminent famine, threatening the lives of over 1.5 million people. Experts attributed the situation on the floods that ravaged the region late this year and the drought which is expected to strike the region early next year.
The experts also blamed insecurity in Gulu, Pader, Kitgum and Amuru for the famine, saying the people were unable to tend their gardens.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Wants Farmers to Grow Cassava for Export
Headline: Rwanda: Minister Wants Farmers to Grow Cassava for Export
Body_Text:
Kigali — Farmers need to improve on the quality and quantity of cassava they produce because government intends to put up processing factories that will add value to have the cassava exported, Agriculture Minister Anastese Murekezi said on Wednesday.
"The supply for cassava is very small locally but the demand both here (in Rwanda) and abroad is very high because Rwandan cassava is very delicious", Murekezi told RNA on the sidelines of stake-holders meeting that is assessing performance of the agriculture sector and map out future interventions.

Original_Search_Title: BoN Sees Potential in Manufacturing Sector
Headline: Namibia: BoN Sees Potential in Manufacturing Sector
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Namibia has the potential to manufacture goods such as pencils, fences, and swimming pool and bathing salts, large-scale production of leather goods, mattresses and fuel production from cassava, says a recent study by the Bank of Namibia (BoN).
Other new products the country could also venture into are metal products and auto components, unique wood, palm and clay products, leisure garments, felts (inner pads for jackets) made from wool as well as the large-scale production of hand-woven carpets.

Original_Search_Title: NEPC Leads 44 Coys to Dubai Trade Fair
Headline: Nigeria: NEPC Leads 44 Coys to Dubai Trade Fair
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) is leading over 44 companies to participate at the Dubai Trade Fair.
The Acting Executive Director and Chief Executive Officer of NEPC, Mr. Aliyu Lawal disclosed this at a press briefing in Abuja that the Council will lead a delegation of 44 Nigerian Companies to participate in the fair otherwise known as Global Village Shopping Festival which holds between December 13th, 2007 and March 1st, 2008.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Express Concern
Headline: Gambia: Farmers Express Concern
Body_Text:
Farmers in the Central River region have expressed concerns over the trade season and constraints affecting their work. This reporter visited both banks of the region and had interviews with farmers.
Speaking to this reporter, Mr. Samba Saye a 65 year old man, said farming then and now have a very big difference. He stated that in the past the Agriculture Department used to help farmers with fertilizers and insecticides for the farming period and facilities to store their produce. "We are doing our best but we have constraints, we have no place to sell our produce,"

Original_Search_Title: He Earns Sh40m a Year From Organic Farming
Headline: Uganda: He Earns Sh40m a Year From Organic Farming
Body_Text:
Kampala — I vowed to live my life organically after realising that it was the best kind of life," says Timothy Njakasi, a farmer in Mukono district. Njakasi does not only live his life organically, he set up a school to train farmers in organic farming practices. Kasenge Riverford Organic Centre, located near Mbalala Trading Centre, 18 miles on the Kampala-Jinja highway, is fully-equipped, with good boarding facilities.
Organic farming is a practice where crops are grown without the use of chemicals like pesticides. Farmers use manure like rotting crop matter, animal dung, urine, ash, etc. The world organic market has grown by 20% in the last 10 years. In 1998, it stood at around $13b (sh22.5t), but grew to around $25b (43.2t) in 2005. At the moment, it is growing at around 10% annually.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Receive Cassava Cuttings
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Receive Cassava Cuttings
Body_Text:
Kampala — KIBAALE district has distributed disease-resistant cassava cuttings to over 5,000 farmers. The production officer, Peter Sentayi, said the cuttings, worth sh30m, were supplied to all sub-counties.
He added that the cassava was resistant to drought. Sentayi added that the project was in line with President Yoweri Museveni's directive to districts to avail improved farming materials to boost food security in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Busia Seeks Sh26m for Tsetse Flies
Headline: Uganda: Busia Seeks Sh26m for Tsetse Flies
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Busia district production department is in need of sh16m to control the outbreak of tsetse flies and the cassava brown-steak virus in the area.
In a report submitted to the district agriculture department, the secretary for production and natural resources, Charles Malowa Kudede, said Bulumbi and Busitema sub-counties were infested with tsetse flies, while other parts of the district had been hit by the cassava brown-steak virus.

Original_Search_Title: Companies in the News
Headline: Ghana: Companies in the News
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana Bans Transit of Foreign Cocoa Beans
Ghana has banned the overland transit of cocoa and cocoa waste from neighbouring states across its territory to stop it being used as a cover for smuggling Ghanaian beans out of the country, states Reuters report.

Original_Search_Title: Dancing African Night Away in New York
Headline: Uganda: Dancing African Night Away in New York
Body_Text:
Kampala — We might be poor, and some people even allege that we are intellectually challenged, but there is at least one thing no one can take away from us: We sure know how to party.
Still, when a friend at the Ugandan Permanent Mission to the United Nations in New York tipped me off that the African Group at the United Nations was organising the first-ever African Night, I wasn't too sure that the event was worth my time. I mean, we are talking about diplomats here.

Original_Search_Title: Hope for Farmers As Project Opens New Income Front
Headline: Kenya: Hope for Farmers As Project Opens New Income Front
Body_Text:
Kenya and Japan governments have embarked on an ambitious farming project targeting 92,000 families in the Kerio Valley region.
The Sh120 million venture will be supervised by the Japanese International Cooperation Agency (JICA) and the Ministry of Agriculture.

Original_Search_Title: Nkoranza Chief Praise Kufuor For Giving Them New District
Headline: Ghana: Nkoranza Chief Praise Kufuor For Giving Them New District
Body_Text:
The Chiefs and people of the newly-created Nkoranza North District have held a durbar at Busunya, the proposed District Capital, to express their profound gratitude and appreciation to President John Agyekum Kufuor for the creation of the new district.
The newly-created Nkoranza North District has 42 communities, which includes Dromankese, Yefri, Kranka, Manso, Pinihin, as well as the famous Fiema and Buabeng, where the monkey sanctuary is located.

Original_Search_Title: Wee - Lucrative Business for Dzemani Children?
Headline: Ghana: Wee - Lucrative Business for Dzemani Children?
Body_Text:
Asikuma — Many school going children at Dzemeni in the South Dayi District of the Volta Region have taken an interest in Indian hemp, popularly known as "wee", production in the area including island communities across the Volta Lake at the expense of their education.
In recent times, records indicate that the junior high schools in the area, performed poorly due to the fact that they were engaged on the wee farms, which have become a viable economic activity to them.

Original_Search_Title: FG Plans Increase Agricultural Production, Exports
Headline: Nigeria: FG Plans Increase Agricultural Production, Exports
Body_Text:
Lagos — FEDERAL Government has disclosed plans to increase agricultural production in the country to enhance service delivery in terms of food availability, accessibility and export.
Dr. Abba Sayyadi Ruman, Minister of Agriculture and Water Resources made the disclosure while speaking at the opening ceremony at the 6th Nigeria Economic Summit Group (NESG) Agricultural Summit in Abuja.

Original_Search_Title: "I Have the Height, Shape And I'm Intelligent And Bold" Isha Sowa
Headline: Sierra Leone: "I Have the Height, Shape And I'm Intelligent And Bold" Isha Sowa
Body_Text:
Freetown — Contestant number six in this week's Miss University Beauty pageant Monday said not only is she smart, but she's also got a nice body. Isha Sowa, 22, said she is not a shy person, but confident and determined to win.
"I am not a stranger to beauty contests," she said, adding that she took second position in Miss Independent among thirteen contestants, and also contested in the Miss Methodist Girls High School (MGHS), where she was crowned Miss MGHS.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Aids Cassava Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Govt Aids Cassava Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOLLOWING the cassava brown streak disease outbreak in Mukono district, the agriculture ministry has given 200 bags of disease tolerant variety to farmers. Doreen Kataama, the district agriculture officer, said the variety would be given to selected large-scale farmers in the area.
"Other farmers will get the cassava cuttings from their colleagues," she said. In April, CBSD attacked plantations in Kasawo and Seeta-Namuganga sub-counties, the major cassava-growing areas in the district.

Original_Search_Title: South West - Farmers Learn Latest Techniques
Headline: Cameroon: South West - Farmers Learn Latest Techniques
Body_Text:
Ekona (Fako) — The Regional Director of the 50-year-old Institute of Agricultural Research for Development (IRAD-Ekona), Dr. Joseph Mafeni Mase, has observed that, in spite of Government's significant investment, food self- sufficiency has not risen commensurately due to Cameroon 's high population growth.
Dr. Mafeni Mase was speaking while he presided at the Open Field Day in Ekona recently. The Day was organized to vulgarise IRAD's expertise in seed production and multiplication. The Regional Director explained that more assistance and realistic approaches in the agricultural sector are still required to attain food security. One main strategy, he said, was to strengthen the seed production and supply sector to optimize yield and encourage self-employment potentials through processing and marketing.

Original_Search_Title: Agip to Partner With FG On Dev...Particularly Millennium Development Goals
Headline: Nigeria: Agip to Partner With FG On Dev...Particularly Millennium Development Goals
Body_Text:
NIGERIAN Agip oil company has said its vision for next year is consistent with Federal Government's development goals and programmes, particularly the Millennium Development Goals, (MDGS), National Economic Empowerment Development Strategy (NEEDS) and the presidential directive on cassava, rice etc.The Vice Chairman/Managing Director of the oil giant, Carlo Russo, who said this at Obie, ONELGA local government area in Rivers State while declaring open the company's annual Green River Project Farmers Day celebration, said they had already singed an agreement with the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) on crop management strategies in plantain/banana and seed yam delivery.
The agreement which he noted would take effect from next year, would promote the interest of farmers in Bayelsa, Delta and Imo states. He sad the Green River project, an initiative of Agip, has been a resounding success as it continues to affect about three thousand farmers in fourteen locations very positively. "We have 208 cooperative societies with a total membership of over five thousand active farmers. These cooperative societies receive formal training in basic book keeping, management and other operational procedures," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Warns Over Exportation of Cassava
Headline: Nigeria: Minister Warns Over Exportation of Cassava
Body_Text:
Minister of Agriculture and Water Resources, Dr. Sayyadi Abba Ruma, has warned that the planned exportation of cassava will be a mirage if locally fabricated cassava tools in Nigeria are not developed.
Dr Ruma said this at the opening ceremony of the Re-training of fabricators on Cassava Processing, Technologies andTraining Workshop for women processors, held at the National Centre for Agricultural Mechanization (NCAM), Ilorin, Kwara State.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria-Cameroon Trade Has to Be Win-Win Affair - Nigerian Official
Headline: Africa: Nigeria-Cameroon Trade Has to Be Win-Win Affair - Nigerian Official
Body_Text:
The Desk Manager of the Nigerian Export Promotion Council, Princess Estelle Igwe, has asserted that the trade relationship between Nigeria and Cameroon has been on a mutual basis, a win-win affair, so to speak. Princess Igwe is in Douala as part of a Nigerian Trade Mission that is to hold discussions with members of the business community in Cameroon from December 11-13 at Sawa Hotel. In an exclusive interview granted The Post at the Nigerian Consulate in Douala recently, Princess Igwe, among other things, stressed that African countries stand to benefit if they trade more among themselves than with Europe and other parts of the world. Excerpts:
The Post: We would like to know what brings you to Cameroon, and could you throw light on the role of the Nigerian Export Promotion Council?

Original_Search_Title: Benue to Fast-Track Ethanol Project
Headline: Nigeria: Benue to Fast-Track Ethanol Project
Body_Text:
Deputy Governor of Benue State Chief Stephen Lawani has renewed the commitment of the state government to help speed-up the automotive biomite ethanol project being sited in the state by the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC).
Lawani who represented Governor Gabriel Suswan at a courtesy visit to NNPC Group Managing Director Engr Abubakar Yar'adua in Abuja Friday said the state government was mindful of the enormous benefits derivable from the project and would do everything to ensure its success.

Original_Search_Title: AGIP to Partner FG On Dev
Headline: Nigeria: AGIP to Partner FG On Dev
Body_Text:
NIGERIAN Agip oil company has said its vision for next year is consistent with Federal Government's development goals and programmes, particularly the Millennium Development Goals, (MDGS), National Economic Empowerment Development Strategy (NEEDS) and the presidential directive on cassava, rice etc
The Vice Chairman/Managing Director of the oil giant, Carlo Russo, who said this at Obie, ONELGA local government area in Rivers State while declaring open the company's annual Green River Project Farmers Day celebration, said they had already singed an agreement with the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) on crop management strategies in plantain/banana and seed yam delivery.

Original_Search_Title: South West Resumes Agro-Pastoral Show Tradition
Headline: Cameroon: South West Resumes Agro-Pastoral Show Tradition
Body_Text:
As the "farmers' jamboree" kicked-off in Kumba, there was much to discover in every facet.
True to its tradition as the bread basket of Cameroon, the package was enviable as all roads led to Kumba for a two-day agro-pastoral show that kicked-off on December 7, 2007. Over 600 exhibition stands were graced with the best in foodstuff, livestock, embroidery and other items produced by stakeholders in the agro-pastoral sector. After Buea in 2001, Menji in 2002, Tombel in 2003, Mamfe in 2004, (no event in 2005), Mundemba in 2006; Kumba (the economic hub of the South West Province) left no stone unturned this year to make what many term "the farmers' feast" or "the farmers' jamboree" a commendable event.

Original_Search_Title: Village Head Pleads for State Assistance
Headline: Zimbabwe: Village Head Pleads for State Assistance
Body_Text:
"WE still sit under the trees . . .I want to ask the authorities among us here if we really deserve this," Jolamnethi Moyo said, plunging the crowd into deep silence.
His sentiments were directed at Christone Maguma, representing Midlands province governor Cephas Msipa, at the recent launch of two HIV and Aids intervention programmes.

Original_Search_Title: Akufo-Addo Will Plunge Country Into Chaos- Alan's Coordinator Alleges in Kumasi
Headline: Ghana: Akufo-Addo Will Plunge Country Into Chaos- Alan's Coordinator Alleges in Kumasi
Body_Text:
MR. MICHAEL Ampong, Campaign Coordinator for Mr. Alan Kyerematen says Ghana will be plunged into chaos should the New Patriotic Party (NPP) delegates consider Nana Addo Danquah Akufo-Addo for the flagbearership slot of the party and eventually elected president.
According to him, the former Foreign Affairs Minister is arrogant, temperamental and hot-headed for the number one job of the land, the Presidency, and therefore, it will be suicidal for the NPP to parade him as its presidential candidate.

Original_Search_Title: Hundreds of Kenyan Tribesmen Stage Raid On Tarime Village
Headline: Tanzania: Hundreds of Kenyan Tribesmen Stage Raid On Tarime Village
Body_Text:
Tarime — Hundreds of Kipsigis tribesmen and militia from Trans Mara district in Kenya, on Tuesday torched farms in Tarime district in an unprovoked attack.
On Wednesday, Tarime district commissioner Stanley Kolimba, confirmed the attack but gave no details.

Original_Search_Title: 'None of Them Can Contest Me' Esther Pabai
Headline: Sierra Leone: 'None of Them Can Contest Me' Esther Pabai
Body_Text:
Freetown — First year female student in the faculty of Arts, Fourah Bay College contesting this year's Miss University beauty pageant, Esther Pabai yesterday said none of her colleagues could contest her in terms of beauty, physical fitness and other prerequisites to grab the crown.
Pabai, 20, said she believes in going for what she wants and for many times she had tried and has been very successful.

Original_Search_Title: U.S.$13 Million Grant Boosts Women in Agriculture
Headline: Kenya: U.S.$13 Million Grant Boosts Women in Agriculture
Body_Text:
A US$13 million pan-African initiative to increase the role of women scientists in agriculture was launched this week (5 December) in Kenya.
The Nairobi-based African Women in Agricultural Research and Development (AWARD) intends to increase the number of women scientists on the continent. It also seeks to provide role models and address the institutional biases that have limited women in agricultural research.

Original_Search_Title: Biofuels - Benefits And Risks for Developing Countries
Headline: Africa: Biofuels - Benefits And Risks for Developing Countries
Body_Text:
Biofuels offer huge potential, but pose challenges best countered with strong and coherent development policies, says S. Arungu-Olende.
Global production of biofuels is growing steadily and will continue to do so. Biofuels offer greater energy security, reduced emissions of greenhouse gases and particulates, rural development, better vehicle performance, and reduced demand for petroleum.

Original_Search_Title: Biofuel - Continent's New Oil?
Headline: Africa: Biofuel - Continent's New Oil?
Body_Text:
Biofuel holds great promise for Africa but the research isn't yet in place to reap the rewards, or analyse the pitfalls, reports Kimani Chege.
It is only ten o'clock in the morning and Kenyan taxi-van driver Richard Kamiri is already tired. Throughout the morning he has had to explain to his passengers the reason he has repeatedly hiked the fare. In just a week, crude oil prices have risen twice -- with the people bearing the costs.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Presents N52bn Budget for 2008
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Presents N52bn Budget for 2008
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Kwara state governor, Dr. Bukola Saraki has presented a total budget of N52, 451,690,991 estimate for year 2008 to the members of the state house of assembly.
Presenting the estimate to the house of assembly on Wednesday in Ilorin, the economic sector took the larger share of the proposed budget estimate with N10, 226,164,136 followed by the Regional Development, which has a total of N7, 722,388,482 representing 26.5 per cent of the budget estimate.

Original_Search_Title: Local Official Brings Power to the People
Headline: Nigeria: Local Official Brings Power to the People
Body_Text:
Kuje, Nigeria — Kevin Aniebonem's voice is difficult to hear over the generator roaring in the background. The 24-year-old buys four liters of petrol every day to keep a light bulb running in his showroom of curtains, drapery, and fabric.
The $10 he spends on fuel is money he has to make up for with higher prices and sales every day.
"If we had power," he says, "business would be moving." The new council chairman in Kuje, the town where Aniebonem lives and works outside Nigeria's capital, Abuja, has promised to bring the district power. A new electrical pole was just erected outside the shop. Now Aniebonem is waiting for light.
Down the street in the Kuje Area Council office, the halls and corridors are jammed by 10am, filled with men, women, and youth like Aniebonem who hope the new government chairman, the Honorable Danladi Etsu Zhin, will hear their case. Tracing the line from the chairman's reception to his office door is easy; just follow the crowd's eager expressions. Every few minutes, his office door opens and another is let in.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
"This is a grass roots office," explains the council's information officer, who identifies herself simply as Mrs. Olaliye. "He can't run away from the people. The door is always open."
Since his election as  council chairman last April, Zhin looks set to meet Aniebonem's and others' expectations. In a district where most have had access to neither electricity nor water in more than 40 years, Zhin started to provide both after just over 100 days in office.
Electrical poles went up in a matter of weeks, stretching from Abuja's airport nearby to Kuje's villages and town center. Newly-purchased tanker trucks have delivered water to communities where boreholes cannot be drilled fast enough. The chairman says that more is planned for his four-year term. "The time is already running short, there is so much we want to do," he says.
Zhin is part of what many Nigerians hope will be a new generation of leadership, led by President Umaru Yar'Adua, a self-proclaimed "servant leader." Seeking to break the pattern set by generations of politicians who looted government treasuries, the President has staked his reputation on following the rule of law and fighting corruption. The violence and irregularities which marred the elections that brought him to power seem to have made him only more determined to succeed.
It is the same with Zhin. A member of the ruling People's Democratic Party, his election as chairman was challenged in a petition to a court, but upheld. Rather than upset his quiet demeanor, the petition seems to have increased his determination to deliver. "This government is for the people; I am just heading it," Zhin says.
Zhin is clear about what Kuje needs: light, health, roads and water are priorities. The Kuje area council is part of the Federal Capital Territory in which Abuja is located, but its roads and buildings bear little resemblance to those of the polished capital city. In Kuje town, commercial business is slowed by unpaved roads which are often impassable except by foot.
Many people from the town work in Abuja's city center but choose to live a 45-minute bus ride away where prices and rent are cheaper. Other residents are farmers, who plant subsistence crops, or cassava and yam to sell in a local market.
The money wasted on generator power is astounding; an electrician in the town estimates that most households spend about $40 a month on petrol while government power would cost only a fraction—as little as $6.50.
Danladi Etsu Zhin, now just 39, grew up in Kuje. He attended the local primary and secondary schools, leaving only to attend college before returning to local politics. Since he came to office, Zhin has been touring his area of jurisdiction.
Down the road from his office in Kuje town, villagers in Kuje Gudaba say he came through some weeks ago to follow up on campaign promises of electricity and water. "We believe him because of what he has shown us," explains civil defense officer Isaac Tyonumi, pointing to new power lines above his home.
In the town's commercial center, men pass cement blocks up a ladder, adding to the near-complete structure of Kuje's first town hall. Now politicians and citizens elsewhere in the capital territory are taking note. Its minister of state, Senator James John Akpanudoedehe, lauded Zhin's performance on a recent visit to Kuje.
Yet not everyone in Kuje is easily convinced. Politicians have made similar promises in the past without result; much of what Zhin now promises has been left undone for over four decades. Unfulfilled contracts, signed only to have payments released, litter Nigeria's budget—particularly in lucrative sectors like infrastructure and power supply.
Zhin says this is exactly what he wants to avoid. In July, This Day newspaper reported that the chairman annulled a contract worth N239m (about $1.9m) after the contractor allegedly left the work site without finishing.
Even if plans for electricity, water, and roads are realized, challenges will remain. Once power lines are up, homes and businesses will have to hire electricians to connect their buildings, something that Kuje residents say costs the prohibitively high sum of N8,000 (about $64).
And there is no guarantee that the government's power grid can supply what is demanded in Kuje. Nigeria, a country of more than 140 million people, produced only about 19 billion kilowatt hours of electricity in 2004, according to the United States government's Energy Information Administration.
The grid cannot maintain a continuous supply of power, nor does it cover the whole country. So desperate is the energy situation that President Yar'Adua has proclaimed a national emergency to resolve the crisis.
Power lines, like the ones going up in Kuje, are a start to delivery, but production remains too low. Joseph Uko, a local electrician, says his business fixing generators has actually gotten better in recent years, even as more people have connected to the national power grid.
Still, Aniebonem says there are signs of movement—something he has rarely seen in Kuje before. He thinks things will improve "if there is a good somebody there." Danladi Etsu Zhin is that somebody for now.
Expectations of Nigeria's new administration are high. At least in this district, progress in delivering on promises is visible. All of Kuje is waiting for results.

Original_Search_Title: November Inflation Down 4.8 Percent
Headline: Uganda: November Inflation Down 4.8 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — INFLATION slipped to 4.8% in the year ending November compared to 5.5% for the year ended October due to lower food prices compared to a year ago, the Uganda Bureau of Statistics has said.
"The reduction was due to lower price levels for food which dropped by 0.19%," a statement from the bureau said.

Original_Search_Title: The Man Who Drove the Queen
Headline: Uganda: The Man Who Drove the Queen
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRADUATION, wedding day, the birth of a child .. Such are the experiences that will forever be special and memorable in many people's lives. But much as some of these experiences are shared, Salim Twalib Khalili's was exceptional - driving the queen of the United Kingdom and Ireland, during the just concluded Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM).
Khalili chauffeured Queen Elizabeth II, who was here to open the November 23-25 CHOGM, together with her husband Phillip, the Prince of Edinburgh.

Original_Search_Title: Fed By the Bus
Headline: Uganda: Fed By the Bus
Body_Text:
Kampala — From drivers and conductors to cleaners and gatemen, dozens of Ugandans depend on the bus industry for their livelihood, Harriet Birungi discovered
RUNNING after a Gaso bus which is just arriving at the Baganda Bus Park in Kampala, Alex Twinamatsiko, a luggage carrier, tries to catch the attention of the alighting passengers. Banging his hand on the side of the bus he calls out:

Original_Search_Title: Aftermath of Chogm
Headline: Uganda: Aftermath of Chogm
Body_Text:
Kampala — NOW that we are post-CHOGM we can get back to 'normal', but what should we judge as normal?
Is traffic chaos normal? Is disposing of plastic bottles and rubbish by throwing them out the car window normal? Are potholes normal? One of the intangible benefits of CHOGM was that it demonstrated to us what life could be like if we tried.

Original_Search_Title: Lifeline for the North As Peace Returns
Headline: Uganda: Lifeline for the North As Peace Returns
Body_Text:
Kampala — Two women return home with their first harvests.
A BABY crawls out of a small mud and bamboo hut sporting a smile, but swiftly disappears back into the hut, when she sees me. A few minutes later, her mother - Grace Achayo - appears from the opposite direction pushing an ox-plough, her two giggling 10-year-old twin daughters, in tow.

Original_Search_Title: A New Publisher, Creating a New Industry
Headline: Nigeria: A New Publisher, Creating a New Industry
Body_Text:
Abuja — When Bibi Bakare-Yusuf holds her two reviewers' copies of Kemi's Journal—one printed in India and one in Nigeria—side by side, the difference is night and day.
Abidemi Sanusi's 2005 release looks vibrant with its red Indian-printed cover depicting Kemi's world—her thoughts, her person, her attitude. The same book, printed in Nigeria, looks a bit dull. The blue hues fade into the surroundings and the thinner paper inside smears slightly from ink that is not quite refined enough.
Cassava Republic, Nigeria's newest literary publishing company, prints in India. Bakare-Yusuf, its founder, says she wants its books to rival those published in London and the United States—not just in literary quality, but in appearance as well.
Cassava Republic is unique in Nigeria, not only for its quality printing but because it exists at all. When Bakare-Yusuf returned to Nigeria after a period of living in the United Kingdom, she was astonished. Literature and its publishers had all but disappeared, leaving religious works, self-help books, and textbooks to fill the shelves.
Keep up with the latest headlines on WhatsApp | LinkedIn
The mood had changed, too. "I found it upsetting," she said. "Someone will spend 5,000 naira on a self-help or management book, but ask them to pay 850 naira for a novel and they will moan and groan."
Nigeria was once home to a flourishing market, but years of military rule pushed international publishers out; not a single one remains in the country today. Economic decline hit the pockets of average Nigerians hard and their budget for luxuries such as books even harder. Thousands from the chattering middle class left the country, including the intellectuals and consumers of Nigerian literature.
"It was the Sahara Desert period of Nigerian literature," says Ken Ike Okere, a co-founder of the Abuja Literary Society. "We didn't know if people were writing because we were not seeing it." Newspapers suffered too; while any of three large papers could sell 500,000 copies a day in the 1970s, today all the papers together are lucky to sell 350,000.
With no retail market and no capital, most publishers now survive by printing textbooks or religious publications, which have guaranteed sales to schools and churches. The market for literature is tougher, and most works that do appear are self-published. Distribution is limited and costly. Most publishers focus on urban centers because the cost of transport and the small market make sales elsewhere unprofitable.
With the exodus of intellectuals and the books they read, many aspiring writers in Nigeria have had no opportunity to learn from their peers. It is no surprise then, that Bakare-Yusuf has struggled to find good manuscripts from the 50 or so she receives every week.
"Think about Yoruba drum training," she says as an example. "It takes years to be a master drummer, years of listening to others, so why should writing be any different?"
Today, Bakare-Yusuf thinks she might just know how to reverse the decline.
"I'm interested in the bottom of the pyramid; the top can go to London to buy books. If I can get books to the masses, conversations about literature will start, a questioning spirit will develop," she explains.
So Cassava Republic is cutting prices and boosting quality. Books like Kemi's Journal that stand out on the shelf cost just 650 naira, compared to the average price of 1,000 naira.
That has meant taking a smaller profit—or none at all. But it's the future market in which Bakare-Yusuf and her company are interested. Once the masses start reading, demand will pick up, she says.
Elite book stores with proper shops are out. Instead, books are being bought in rackety shops outside city walls. In Abuja, where Cassava Republic is based, that means leaving the tidy streets of the capital and heading for the suburbs, where most people live in mud and metal houses. It is on the winding streets of Maraba, Kuje, and Nyanya villages that Nigerians are buying books.
Cassava Republic's raw material is different also. Its books are new, catchy, and accessible. Sanusi is just one of a handful of writers that Bakare-Yusuf has found herself. Another, Teju Cole, author of the forthcoming Every Day is for The Thief, had a blog that caught the company's attention.
And while some titles, including Cain Prize winner Helon Habila and Orange Prize winner Diana Evans, are reprinted from abroad, Cassava Republic has turned down offers from international publishers to reprint.
"I'm interested in authors who engage with the African experience—it's not enough just to have an African author," she explains. "They must be able to speak to the complexities of African realities." Soon, Cassava Republic hopes to re-publish The Hidden Star, by the young South African author, K. Sello Duiker.
Someday soon, more of those authors may come from Nigeria itself. As part of her work, Bakare-Yusuf invites authors whose manuscripts are turned down to take part in writing workshops and join a mailing list of tips and events. Elsewhere in Nigeria, others are doing the same. The Abuja Literary Society also edits manuscripts and helps authors with their work.
All this, says Bakare-Yusuf, is about getting deeper than literature: "When you ask someone to read literature, you are asking them not only to look into the past but to look into the future—to suspend their beliefs today." That just might help Nigeria think its way out of more than just the end of a novel.

Original_Search_Title: "The Blood Keeps Flowing"
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: "The Blood Keeps Flowing"
Body_Text:
Masisi — Semivumbi Ntawiheba never used to be interested in politics. Growing up on a mountain farm close to Masisi town in the Democratic Republic of Congo's restive North Kivu, Ntawiheba lived only for his cattle.
Then, two months ago, soldiers loyal to dissident General Laurent Nkunda attacked his village and took away some of the men aged between 12 and 50 for "conscription". "I didn't want to be one of them," Ntawiheba says. "So I decided to flee."

Original_Search_Title: North West - Haman Adama Best School Farm Project Launched
Headline: Cameroon: North West - Haman Adama Best School Farm Project Launched
Body_Text:
Bamenda (Mezam)- The sum total is to inspire and introduce agriculture to youths as a noble and highly rewarding occupation. The North West Delegation of Basic Education is to thank for initiating the "Haman Adama Prize for Excellence in Agriculture" with the declared intention to establish a good and strong agricultural education programme for primary schools in the province.
The provincial Delegate, Boma Nde Soh Christopher told Cameroon Tribune that the Prize will stimulate interest in agriculture, mobilize schools to play active roles in the promotion of Agriculture, develop positive attitudes towards agriculture, prepare children for a career in agriculture, develop a scientific outlook and make schools in the province more productive.

Original_Search_Title: South West - IRAD Showcases Latest Farming Techniques
Headline: Cameroon: South West - IRAD Showcases Latest Farming Techniques
Body_Text:
The Regional Director of the 50-year-old Institute of Agricultural Research for Development (IRAD-Ekona), Dr. Joseph Mafeni Mase, has observed that, in spite of Government significant investment, food self- sufficiency has not been commensurately due to Cameroon's high population growth.
Dr. Mafeni Mase was speaking while presiding at the Open Field Day in Ekona recently. The Day was organized to vulgarise IRAD's expertise in seed production and multiplication. The Regional Director explained that more assistance and realistic approaches in the agricultural sector are still required to attain food security. One main strategy, he said, was to strengthen the seed production and supply sector to optimize yield and encourage self-employment potentials through processing and marketing.

Original_Search_Title: NGO Cares for Cerebral Palsy Kids
Headline: Zambia: NGO Cares for Cerebral Palsy Kids
Body_Text:
Ndola — BOY Bwalya does not know any of the young children in the neighbourhood because he is always indoors at his parents' home in Ndola.
When his parents receive visitors, Bwalya who is only five years old is locked up in the bedroom until the visitors leave.

Original_Search_Title: NNDC Gets N5 Billion Fresh Capital
Headline: Nigeria: NNDC Gets N5 Billion Fresh Capital
Body_Text:
Kaduna — The once colossal New Nigeria Development Company [NNDC] is set for a comeback as the Northern Governors Forum (NGF) has approved its recapitalization to the tune of N5 billion.
NNDC, founded in the 1950s by the old Northern Regional government, is owned by the 19 Northern states today.

Original_Search_Title: N70 Billion Textile Fund - 300 Companies Queue Up
Headline: Nigeria: N70 Billion Textile Fund - 300 Companies Queue Up
Body_Text:
Abuja — More than 300 textile companies have signified interest to borrow from the Federal Government's N70 billion Textile Rehabilitation Fund, the Nigeria Export Import Bank's (NEXIM) Executive Director (Operations) Mr. Edet Ekere, said in Abuja yesterday. He spoke to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).
"The response to our publication in the news-papers for expression of interest to draw from the fund was tremendous, with more than 300 expressions of interest received by NEXIM," Ekere said.

Original_Search_Title: Country of True Wonder And Beauty
Headline: Uganda: Country of True Wonder And Beauty
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA, a land gifted with breathtaking sights, is popularly referred to as the Pearl of Africa. It is described as a place gifted by nature, hospitable people with over 40 tribes with distinct cultural attributes, 10 national parks, warm climate and mind-blowing landscapes. Raphael Okello explores some of the attractions, arguably referred to as Uganda's wonders.
NATURAL WONDERS

Original_Search_Title: The Deserted Village - a Casualty of North Kivu's Continuing Conflict
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: The Deserted Village - a Casualty of North Kivu's Continuing Conflict
Body_Text:
Kimoka — It may only be a short walk from its bustling neighbour Sake, but Kimoka is a ghost town. Homes are abandoned, the school is deserted and the brand new church empty. Light streams in through the open door onto huge leather drums beside the pulpit. When the congregation fled, the drums became redundant. Now the only percussion comes from the small arms fire echoing through the mountains.
Outside, dirty scraps of cloth and one child's shoe are strewn across the black volcanic earth - signs that there was once a community here. Now, there is nothing. No children laughing, no dogs barking.

Original_Search_Title: I Will Win 60 Percent of Votes
Headline: Ghana: I Will Win 60 Percent of Votes
Body_Text:
Accra — One of the leading contenders in the ruling New Patriotic Party (NPP) presidential race, Mr Alan Kojo Kyerematen, has stated that he would sweep over 60% of the votes at their December 22 Congress in Accra. "I say this with all humility that anybody who knows my track record, background, will confirm that when I talk confidently like this, I know why I am saying that."
Though Alan does not rule out a possible alliance with other candidates should there be a run-off, he strongly believes he has what it takes to win in the first round. To him, "there is ample evidence which assures me that I am going to win the race. Opinion polls have put me ahead of my opponents," he said. At a maiden media interaction in Takoradi, Mr Alan Kyerematen popularly referred to as 'Alan Cash' noted that his government would promote grass root capitalism where wealth would be created for the majority to enjoy. "The issue of poverty will be a thing of the past under Alan's presidency," he noted, adding that his leadership would transform Ghana's economy. He contended that he had what it takes to catapult Ghana to a middle-income status.

Original_Search_Title: President's Initiative Transforms Farmers
Headline: Uganda: President's Initiative Transforms Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — RUHARO Parish is located a few kilometres from Bushenyi town. It has 933 households, of which 650 are participating in the Presidential Initiative Against Hunger and Poverty. The farmers have used the little initial capital from the President so well that one is left lamenting about the poor attitude of some of our people towards work. A big section of Ugandans would rather hang along Kampala streets than engage in farming in the village.
Ruharo farmers were poor with barely enough food to feed their families. Now, they boast of too much to eat and enough for the market. They have transformed their lives. Under the initiative, the Poverty Alleviation Department headed by Mrs. Joan Kakwenzire, the Senior Presidential Advisor on Poverty Alleviation, started working with the parish to improve food production, homes, soil fertility and household incomes. Several items were distributed including 60,000 cassava cuttings, 60,000 sweet potato vines, 6,000kgs of maize, 330,000 cabbage seedlings, 330,000 onion seedlings, 450kgs of millet, piglets and 77 cows. Subsequently, the farmers engaged in zero grazing cows, pigs, goats, rabbits and poultry. They also engaged in growing bananas, beans, pineapples, avocados, onions and green vegetables.

Original_Search_Title: PDP Has Robbed Citizens, Says Senator Matori
Headline: Nigeria: PDP Has Robbed Citizens, Says Senator Matori
Body_Text:
Bauchi — A strong member of the ANPP national board of trustee and former member of the upper chamber of the national assembly in the 4th republic,senator Salisu Musa Matori has attributed the current political and economic wranglings in the country, to the poor leadership style of the PDP in the last eight years led by Olusegun Aremu Obasanjo.
Senator Salisu Matori disclosed this to LEADERSHIP correspondent in Bauchi in an interview where he said PDP has seriously demonstrated high sense of poor leadership in the last eight years of their governance,hence the decision of many people to dump the party.

Original_Search_Title: No Pieces to Pick in Teso After Floods As Minister Speaks of Hope
Headline: Uganda: No Pieces to Pick in Teso After Floods As Minister Speaks of Hope
Body_Text:
Katakwi — I recently travelled to Palam village, Ngariam Sub-County in Katakwi District bordering Moroto District. To reach Soroti from Mbale, I crossed Awoja Bridge by boat. The water had not completely subsided. It was a bit of fun. On either side of the bridge, taxis waited for passengers.
From Soroti to Katakwi, the road is now dry and dusty. I could see the havoc caused by floods on bridges along that road. Some bridges were swept deep into the swamps.

Original_Search_Title: People Consuming Less - Museveni
Headline: Africa: People Consuming Less - Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — UNDER-consumption is the greatest enemy of business in Africa, President Yoweri Museveni has said. He told a gathering of hundreds of senior executives from companies like Coca-Cola and Chevron, in South Africa, that if all the 900 million people in Africa had reached average consumption of all commodities and services, investors would be richer.
Museveni made the remarks on Friday at a dinner hosted in his by the Corporate Council on Africa in his honour and that of other dignitaries who attended the US-Africa Business Summit that ended yesterday in Cape Town.

Original_Search_Title: PPP As Tonic for Niger's Development
Headline: Nigeria: PPP As Tonic for Niger's Development
Body_Text:
After 27 years of statehood, Niger State is yet to be set on a steady path to real development. Practical indices of development reveal that the state has either continually stagnated or has often been driven back to the starting point at the end of the tenures of successive administrations.
Nothing indeed underscores the bad state of things in the state than the practical breakdown of virtually all public utilities. A careful analysis of the situation shows that much of the problem lies with the sad departure from the conventional ways of conducting the business of governance. Sustained through the successive dispensations, the bitter trend had over time thrown the polity into serious socio-moral crisis.

Original_Search_Title: "Book Minister" Inaugurates Read Campaign Committee
Headline: Nigeria: "Book Minister" Inaugurates Read Campaign Committee
Body_Text:
The Minister of State for Education (I), Dr. Anthony Jerry Agada on Tuesday 13th November inaugurated a 24-man National Action Committee on Read Project (NACORP).
In his opening remarks before the inauguration, the Minister commended the committee for their commitment and all the work they have done so far, from the conception of the project which is one of the Ministry's reform programme till date. He charged the committee not to rest on their oars but to deliberate extensively on strategies to employ to advance the course of the Read Project. Recounting his own experience as Permanent Secretary in Benue State where he had to give the needed support to all activities geared towards improving the reading culture, the "Book Minister as he is now fondly called reiterated the Ministry's desire to give the committee a 100% support to achieve their objectives. Dr. Mrs. Makoju, a Director in the Ministry in her address informed the committee that its activities will not be driven completely by the Ministry.

Original_Search_Title: Taming the Garrison Commander
Headline: Nigeria: Taming the Garrison Commander
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yesterday, the self-styled strongman of Ibadan politics, Chief Lamidi Ariyibi Adedibu, was held by the Police and quizzed for about two hours.
He was subsequently charged to Abuja Magistrate Court in Abuja for alleged breach of public peace. He was granted bail and released.

Original_Search_Title: LWF/WS Empowers Many in Bong
Headline: Liberia: LWF/WS Empowers Many in Bong
Body_Text:
The Lutheran World Federation Department of World Service (LWF/WS), with support from an international church-based organization in Finland, (FINN CHURCH AID), has empowered thousands of citizens and residents of Panta and Zota Districts in Bong County to carry out activities in the areas of agriculture, and the construction of bridges, hand pumps, latrines, among others.
The citizens and residents, who spoke to the press in Bong County, praised the Lutheran World Federation/World Service and the FINN CHURCH AID for empowering them to be able to carry out these activities, which now serve as sources of livelihood for them.

Original_Search_Title: Measures to Ease Fertilizer Distribution Identified
Headline: Nigeria: Measures to Ease Fertilizer Distribution Identified
Body_Text:
Abuja — The difficulties associated with the fertilizer supply programme of the federal ministry of agriculture in Nigeria will soon become a thing of the past as some measures have been put in place to prevent the problems encountered during the 2007 season exercise.
These measures, according to reliable source from the ministry, include timely procurement process when the international prices of fertilizers are less competitive, timely deliveries before the raining season in order to ease road transportation problems, tangible funding arrangement with payment to contractors, selection of few, competent and reliable companies that have good records of performance over the years to execute the contract.

Original_Search_Title: Environmental Law Court Arrests 10 in Kwara
Headline: Nigeria: Environmental Law Court Arrests 10 in Kwara
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Ten persons believed to have violated environmental law in Kwara State were recently arrested and fined N1, 000 each .
The state government had recently banned street trading in unhygienic places in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Why Kampala is the Heart of Illegal Ivory Trade
Headline: Uganda: Why Kampala is the Heart of Illegal Ivory Trade
Body_Text:
Kampala — UWA enforcement unit supervisor Steven Kimpwedde displays ivory pieces estimated at sh277m at their head offices. They were recovered by UWA in December 2006
ONLY elephants are supposed to wear ivory, so goes a popular slogan. But Wang Xiuli, a youthful Chinese, disrespected it. She was caught smuggling ivory recently. Wang was restless as customs officials checked her overweight bag in Busia.

Original_Search_Title: Niger Govt Builds N37.5b Ethanol Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Govt Builds N37.5b Ethanol Plant
Body_Text:
Niger — Niger State Government has signed a N37.5 billion agreement with OSPREY Investment Company Ltd for the construction of an ethanol factory in the state.
The state deputy governor, Hon. Ahmed Ibeto, while signing the agreement on behalf of the state government, said the agreement was in furtherance of the state's industrial policy, which places a high premium on public-private-partnership for the rapid socio-economic development of the state.

Original_Search_Title: Government Gives Shs50m to Kayunga Storm Victims
Headline: Uganda: Government Gives Shs50m to Kayunga Storm Victims
Body_Text:
Kayunga — THE Ministry of Disaster Preparedness has donated relief items worth Shs50m to residents in Kayunga District whose homes and crops were ravaged by a storm.
The items, which included 470 bags of maize flour, 200 bags of beans and 250 bags of improved cassava cuttings.

Original_Search_Title: U.S Spends $15m Annually On Country's Agriculture -Mission Director
Headline: Nigeria: U.S Spends $15m Annually On Country's Agriculture -Mission Director
Body_Text:
The United States Agency for International Development (USAID) spends over $15 million annually on Nigeria's agriculture, the agency's mission director has said.
Sharon Cromer made the remarks weekend when she visited the Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources Abuja. She said USAID is investing the money as Nigeria's development partner to improve the livelihoods of the citizens.

Original_Search_Title: Why Mechanisation of Agriculture Still Remains a Dream
Headline: Uganda: Why Mechanisation of Agriculture Still Remains a Dream
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Government's call for modernisation of agriculture is stilla myth. Mechanisation cannot be achieved when farmers are still using hoes and relying on rainfall to work on their farms," the Mukono district LC5 chairman, Francis Lukooya observes.
Recently, Pastor Samuel Kakande unveiled state-of-the-art agricultural equipment worth sh3b.

Original_Search_Title: Grinding Mill Products Cause Cancer - Study
Headline: Uganda: Grinding Mill Products Cause Cancer - Study
Body_Text:
Kampala — CONSUMPTION of groundnuts, maize, cassava chips, soybeans and millet processed by local grinding mills could lead to cancer, a Usaid funded research done by Makerere University has shown.
Dr Archileo Kaaya, a senior lecturer in the Department of Food Science and Technology at the university, conducted the research from 2000-2007 across the country.

Original_Search_Title: Model Cassava Plant for Training of Officials
Headline: Nigeria: Model Cassava Plant for Training of Officials
Body_Text:
A model cassava processing plant currently under construction in Karu, Nasarawa, will serve as a training facility for stakeholders in cassava processing, according to an official source.
The source at the Ministry of Commerce and Industry, owners of the plant, told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) yesterday in Abuja that the plant was 95 per cent completed.

Original_Search_Title: Purging  Peanuts of Deadly Aflatoxin
Headline: Malawi: Purging  Peanuts of Deadly Aflatoxin
Body_Text:
Local efforts to put an end to aflatoxin outbreaks are helping groundnut farmers back to prosperity, reports Charles Mkoka.
For Amos Katosa, a subsistence farmer in Malawi's central region district of Mchinji, the groundnut -- popularly known as the peanut-- has been his main source of income for the past 40 years.

Original_Search_Title: Smallscale Farmers to Benefit From Peanut Plant
Headline: Kenya: Smallscale Farmers to Benefit From Peanut Plant
Body_Text:
Small scale farmers from Nyanza are set to benefit from the establishment of a peanut processing factory in the region.
The plant has already been set up at the offices of Mich, a community based organisation in Kisumu.

Original_Search_Title: Chinese Team to Discuss Agricultural Centre
Headline: Mozambique: Chinese Team to Discuss Agricultural Centre
Body_Text:
Maputo — A Chinese technical team will shortly visit Maputo to negotiate the final details for the project to build an Agricultural Technology and Development Centre.
An agreement in principle to set up such a centre was reached during the visit of Chinese President Hu Jintao to Mozambique in February. The initial idea was to establish the centre in the northern province of Nampula, but it was subsequently decided to move it to Boane in Maputo province.

Original_Search_Title: Muhwezi Trial - Beyond the Prosecution
Headline: Uganda: Muhwezi Trial - Beyond the Prosecution
Body_Text:
The trial is like the field court martial of 1981--86 where the comrades compare contribution in a particular battle, ask why an individual is being punished for running away with some cassava tuber he uprooted yet the other who took a village girl and some chicken has been let free
When the Inspector General of Government (IGG), Justice Faith Mwondha, walked into the chief magistrates court on Buganda Road Monday last week, she was sure she had plugged the holes in her first major trial.

Original_Search_Title: Border Opens for Country Maize
Headline: Namibia: Border Opens for Country Maize
Body_Text:
Windhoek — The closed border period between Namibia and Zambia opened last month with Namibia's largest miller, Namib Mills importing thousands of tonnes of maize from that country.
It is the first time that Namibia is importing maize from Zambia in huge quantities following Zambia's bumper harvest last season.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture, Pivot for Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture, Pivot for Devt
Body_Text:
Lagos — The latest World Development Report calls for greater investment in agriculture in developing countries and warns that the sector must be placed at the center of the development agenda if the goals of halving extreme poverty and hunger by 2015 are to be realised.
Titled 'Agriculture for Development', the report says the agricultural and rural sectors have suffered from neglect and under-investment over the past 20 years. While 75 percent of the world's poor live in rural areas, a mere 4 percent of official development assistance goes to agriculture in developing countries.

Original_Search_Title: Country, China to Boost Trade Ties
Headline: Nigeria: Country, China to Boost Trade Ties
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) has said that the provision of accurate trade statistics between Nigeria and China will enable the Council focus more on specific products for export to China.
The acting executive director and chief executive officer, Mr. Aliyu M. Lawal stated this in Abuja during a courtesy visit by the Economic and Commercial Counselor of the Chinese Embassy, Mr. Huang Jie.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Says Govt on Track on Agriculture
Headline: Rwanda: Government On Track in Agriculture Transformation - World Bank
Body_Text:
Kigali — African governments are spending too little on agriculture and to some extent wastefully - a trend that needs to change if the sector is to be an engine to growth. However, Rwanda is way ahead in prioritizing expenditures in the same sector that other countries need to learn from, a World Bank official said on Tuesday.
Mr. Michael Morris - who was presenting findings of the 2008 World Development Report, told officials from government and donor agencies that despite agriculture contributing significantly to revenue, Africa is spending just under 4% on it. Mr. Michael was part of the team of researchers for the report.

Original_Search_Title: Abia Govt Partners Research Institute
Headline: Nigeria: Abia Govt Partners Research Institute
Body_Text:
Abuja — Abia State government has expressed its willingness to partner with the National Root Crops Research Institute (NRCRI) Umudike to boost agriculture in the state.
Commissioner for Agriculture, Dr (Mrs) Ori Obasi, gave this indication when she paid a visit to the institute recently.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja Farmers Decry Late Govt Assistance
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja Farmers Decry Late Govt Assistance
Body_Text:
Farmers in Abuja have decried the manner in which government attend to their farming needs late.
The farmers spoke to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) under the umbrella of the Federal Capital Agro-Input Dealers Association (FECAIDA).

Original_Search_Title: Malnutrition - Silent Child Killer in Gulu
Headline: Uganda: Malnutrition - Silent Child Killer in Gulu
Body_Text:
Gulu — THREE decades ago, malnutrition related conditions like kwashiorkor, marasmus, starvation in children and adults were minimal.
Foods like millet, yams, cassava, sweet potato, shear nut oil, vegetables such as 'Malakwang', peas, beans, meat, honey, simsim and groundnuts paste among others were in plenty.

Original_Search_Title: Ogun Broadens Farmers' Knowledge On Cash Crops
Headline: Nigeria: Ogun Broadens Farmers' Knowledge On Cash Crops
Body_Text:
As part of measures to give prominence to six cash crops including Cocoa , Cotton, Cassava, Rice, Oil Palm and Kolanut, the Ogun State government has put together a workshop to further broaden the knowledge of farmers about the crops.
Declaring open the workshop with the theme:" Sustaining the State Leadership in Long Staple Cotton Production in Nigeria,' the state commissioner for agriculture, Dr. Iziaq Kunle Salako, highlighted the importance of the training and its timeliness in helping to resuscitate the ailing textile industry.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Recede But Life Still a Challenge
Headline: Uganda: Floods Recede But Life Still a Challenge
Body_Text:
Kampala — "DURING the three months of heavy rains and floods I was left in this hut. I would have been washed away by the floods but I am alive due to God's mercy," says the 88 year-old in Ateso. Susana Eyakolit could not commute to dry areas like the others in her village because she is not only old but also blind.
"She can't walk easily and at times we have to cross small swamps at high risk. So we have no option but to leave her behind and check on her in the mornings," says her brother Kupalyana Awupal, 77.

Original_Search_Title: New Outfit Pulls in Crowds Through Proper Play Choice
Headline: Kenya: New Outfit Pulls in Crowds Through Proper Play Choice
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Festival of Creative Arts seems to have settled well into their new home, the Alliance Francaise auditorium in Nairobi, if the full houses they have been pulling are any yardsticks.
But more importantly, the organisers have not lost the art of selecting good comedies such as Robin Hawdon's Birthday Suite, which opened Friday and ends Saturday.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuff Prices Increase
Headline: Rwanda: Foodstuff Prices Increase
Body_Text:
Kigali — Consumers in Kigali City are digging deep into their pockets to afford meals as prices of foodstuffs have increased, some by 40 per cent.
A mini survey curried out in some markets and shops indicates that peas now cost Frw1,400 from Frw 1200. While groundnuts have hiked to Frw700 from Frw500, this is the highest price city consumers are paying for foodstuffs.

Original_Search_Title: NGO Aims to Produce 18 Million Tonnes of Rice for Continent
Headline: Africa: GTZDO Sets to Produce 18 Million Tonnes of Rice for Continent
Body_Text:
Global Trade Zone Development Organisation, (GTZDO), a non governmental organization under its Global Food production initiative has decided to venture into agriculture, cultivates rice and expects to get about 18million tons of rice for Nigeria and African Market. Mr. Chris Timehin, a Mechanical and Chemical Engineer with local and oversea training and the vice President of the organization spoke to Vanguard on how they intend to dismantle food shortage in Nigeria and Africa.
Excerpts.

Original_Search_Title: Cost of Living Shoots Up
Headline: Uganda: Cost of Living Shoots Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE cost of living rose faster in October than at any time since the beginning of the year and is likely to stay up following increases in prices of staple foods, which constitute the largest percentage of consumer expenditure.
An official of the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) told journalists at a press briefing on Tuesday that when the cost of living goes up, people are forced to adjust their budgets and in the end they spend more than they were doing previously to cope up with increasing prices of goods and services.

Original_Search_Title: Catholic Church Organizes Forum for the Salvation of Northern Economy
Headline: Ghana: Catholic Church Organizes Forum for the Salvation of Northern Economy
Body_Text:
The Catholic Church of Ghana Ecclesiastic Province Pastoral Conference (TEPPCON) has organized a forum for the three northern regions aimed at mapping out a strategy to bridge the gap between the north and south.
The Dean of student University for development studies Professor Saa Dittoh in his address said it is a fallacy that Northern Ghana contributes marginally to Ghana's economic development.

Original_Search_Title: Why a Research Institute Becomes a Bakery
Headline: Rwanda: Why a Research Institute Becomes a Bakery
Body_Text:
If you work in agri-business and want to diversify your products, the Rwanda Agro-Science Institute might give you some ideas.
These days, "producing for export" is the mantra to be sung for entrepreneurs who want to make their business bigger. This, however might be a big step for a micro of start-up enterprise. Their best bet might rather be diversification, all the more so since, for a lot of products, the local market is still largely untapped.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rate Increases to 5.5 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Rate Increases to 5.5 Percent
Body_Text:
Kampala — RISING food prices pushed the October inflation rate to 5.5% from 5% in September, the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS) has said.
"Food prices went up in most centres with significant contributions coming from Matooke, Irish potatoes, cassava, fruits and vegetables like passion fruits, pineapples, cabbages, tomatoes and dried beans," the monthly consumer price index from UBOS said.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Leave Thousands On Verge of Starvation
Headline: Uganda: Floods Leave Thousands On Verge of Starvation
Body_Text:
In Katakwi, Soroti and Bukedea districts, several affected people say they have never received any food relief despite the fact that the heavy rains destroyed their crops
Before the floods hit his village, Amuge in Amuria District, Mzee Fasinu Okumur, 77 was a happy man. His family could afford two meals a day. But after his groundnuts, cassava and sorghum crops were destroyed by the floods, his family hardly gets what to eat.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava to Fight Famine
Headline: Uganda: Cassava to Fight Famine
Body_Text:
Kampala — NAKAPIRIPIRIT district has embarked on multiplication and distribution of cassava stems to farmers to ensure food security.
The National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) coordinator, Simon Okello, recently said 65 acres of cassava multiplication plots had been established in various sub-counties.

Original_Search_Title: Become a Millionaire Through Pig-Rearing
Headline: Uganda: Become a Millionaire Through Pig-Rearing
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE demand for pork is steadily growing, making pig-rearing a lucrative business in the country. Several pork joints have opened in the city and upcountry, leading to the rise in the price of the commodity. A kilogramme of pork now goes for between sh3,500 and 4,500, up from sh2,800 a few months ago.
According to dealers, the supply is low due to a variety of problems. The main problem is lack of a fully commercialised pig-rearing culture. According to statistics from the Ministry of Agriculture, there are about six million pigs countrywide.

Original_Search_Title: 35kg Water Melon Excites Show Goers
Headline: Uganda: 35kg Water Melon Excites Show Goers
Body_Text:
Kampala — A water melon weighing about 35kg was one of the items exhibited on Saturday at Amudat Trading Centre during the World Food Day celebrations for Nakapiripirit district. Lokoona Keebu, a farmers group supported by the National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS) in Kamaturu village, Lorengedwat sub-county, said they picked just one fruit for the exhibition.
"We have even bigger ones. If it was not for the weight, we would have brought bigger ones," the chairperson, Andrew Lokee said.

Original_Search_Title: IFAD Invests $200m On Rural Poverty Schemes
Headline: Nigeria: IFAD Invests $200m On Rural Poverty Schemes
Body_Text:
Abuja — International Fund for Agricultural Development has invested about $200million in projects and programmes aimed at alleviating rural poverty, since Nigeria started using IFAD loan and grant facilities in 1985.
The Fund has also approved over $70 million for two new programmes, namely the Rural Finance Institution-Building Programme (RUFIN) and the Rural Micro-Enterprise Programme (RUMEDP).

Original_Search_Title: Swedes Eye Energy Sector
Headline: Rwanda: Swedes Eye Energy Sector
Body_Text:
Kigali — ABB, a Swedish firm and international leader in power and automation technologies is scouting Rwanda for investment opportunities in the energy sector.
Officials from the ABB Group of companies that operates in about 100 countries and employs 100,000 people were in Kigali last week together with a group of Swedish investors seeking opportunities in the energy sector.

Original_Search_Title: UN Agriculture Fund Announces New Project in Gabon
Headline: Gabon: UN Agriculture Fund Announces New Project in Gabon
Body_Text:
New York — The United Nations International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) today announced the launch of a new project aimed at helping farmers in Gabon diversify their incomes by developing and marketing new products from staple crops.
The agreement for the $5.7 million loan and $300,000 grant was signed in Rome today and will receive co-financing from the OPEC Fund for International Development, IFAD said. "The Gabon economy has been heavily dependent on the oil and timber sectors for some time," said Abdoul Barry, IFAD's country programme manager for Gabon. "This project will offer concrete opportunities to do that by helping small farmers and their organizations get better access to value chains for products with significant market potential," said Mr. Barry of the effort to help market products from crops like banana, cassava and peanut.

Original_Search_Title: Assembly Outlines Programme to Discourage Youth
Headline: Ghana: Assembly Outlines Programme to Discourage Youth
Body_Text:
Accra — To curtail the growing of marijuana, the Tain District Assembly in the Brong-Ahafo Region is providing alternative livelihood for the youth in the district.
The Assembly is offering free tractor ploughing services during the farming season and has drawn up a scheme to supply inputs on credit to the youth for the cultivation of cash crops. The executive committee of the Assembly outlined the new initiatives at its second session meeting at Nsawkaw.

Original_Search_Title: Rakai's Aids Affected Get Seeds
Headline: Uganda: Rakai's Aids Affected Get Seeds
Body_Text:
Rakai — Ms Scovia Nnassali and Ms Clevencia Nakate were among over 150 residents who braved hot weather in Kibaale Village on October 22 for the distribution of seeds and cassava stumps.
It was a long wait and many residents sought shelter on the verandas of the few shop buildings. A mini-bus, loaded with maize, beans, and ground nuts seeds as well as cassava stumps was packed in front of the chairman's office.

Original_Search_Title: Abandoned Bridge Makes Businesses Suffer in Sapele
Headline: Nigeria: Abandoned Bridge Makes Businesses Suffer in Sapele
Body_Text:
Lagos — Is it not ludicrous that government would embark on a project to assuage the suffering of the people, only for the said predicament it set out to ameliorate be multiplied times over by acts of omission and commission of the same government?
This is exactly the question the people of Sapele in Delta State have been asking themselves since the state government, three years ago, 2004 to be precise, awarded a contract for the construction of a multi-culvert bridge on the main strategic link road in the town, only to increase their woes by abandoning the project.

Original_Search_Title: Fadama Projects Have Succeeded in Fct - Agric Sec
Headline: Nigeria: Fadama Projects Have Succeeded in Fct - Agric Sec
Body_Text:
Over 30,000 farmers across the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) are beneficiaries of the various projects of the United Nations, (UN), counterpart funding in the Fadama 1 and 2 projects throughout the territory.
In an interview with LEADERSHIP, the agricultural secretary in the FCT administration, Alhaji Sidi Ado Ali, revealed that the farmers are engaged in the production of various foodstuffs including rice, yams, beans, cassava guinea corn, millet, etc.

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortages Follow Drought, Floods
Headline: Ghana: Food Shortages Follow Drought, Floods
Body_Text:
Dakar — After an initial push to provide food for people affected by drought and heavy flooding in northern Ghana, donor attention on the country is waning even though food shortages persist meaning the situation could get much worse, the Ghanaian government and aid agencies warn.
"Because of the preceding drought and the end of the planting season, there is an inevitable situation of food insecurity, which is likely to last until the next harvest," the International Federation of the Red Cross (IFRC) wrote in a 9 October appeal.

Original_Search_Title: Aids Stripping Farmers of Their Land
Headline: Benin: Aids Stripping Farmers of Their Land
Body_Text:
Cotonou — Comlan Houessou certainly knows what he is talking about when it comes to the impact of AIDS on rural communities. He is a farmer in Benin who has lost everything because of HIV: the respect of his neighbours, his savings and his land. He is now fighting to rebuild his life.
Just five years ago, Houessou had two hectares of land in the Couffo region of southwest Benin. He inherited the land from his family and grew corn, cassava and cotton on it to meet the needs of his two wives and their six children.

Original_Search_Title: House Committee Urges Research Institute to Create Jobs
Headline: Nigeria: House Committee Urges Research Institute to Create Jobs
Body_Text:
The House of Representatives committee on agriculture has urged the National Root Crops Research Institute (NRCRI), Umudike, to see how it can add greater value to the economy of the nation and create more jobs to make Nigerian youths self reliant.
Chairman of the House committee on agriculture, Mr. Gbenga Makuojuola, stated this when he led six other members on a familiarisation visit to the institute. He expressed deep satisfaction with the achievements recorded by the institute so far, adding that the desire of the committee was to ensure that food was put on the table of the ordinary Nigerian.

Original_Search_Title: Neglecting Agriculture - Part One
Headline: Africa: Neglecting Agriculture, 1
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — "The central finding of the study is that the agriculture sector has been neglected by both governments and the donor community, including the World Bank. ..The Bank's limited and, until recently, declining support for addressing the constraints on agriculture has not been used strategically to meet the diverse needs of a sector that requires coordinated intervention across a range of activities." - World Bank Independent Evaluation Group
In its World Development Report for 2008, released on October 19 and entitled "Agriculture for Development," the World Bank stressed the importance of a renewed emphasis on agriculture. The report argues that "for the poorest people, GDP growth originating in agriculture is about four time more effective in reducing poverty than GDP growth originating outside the sector.

Original_Search_Title: IDPs Urged to Be Self-Reliant
Headline: Uganda: IDPs Urged to Be Self-Reliant
Body_Text:
Gulu — Local leaders in northern Uganda have been urged to mobilise and encourage internally displaced persons (IDPs) to be self-reliant instead of depending on humanitarian agencies.
Speaking at a meeting with local leaders recently, the programme officer of the Danish Refugee Council, Bosco Ezangu, said local leaders were not encouraging people to grow food crops. He said they would establish 20 fields in selected sub-counties in Gulu and Amuru districts as model farms from which IDPs would learn modern farming methods. The programme is supported by the Food and Agricultural Organisation and $50,000 has been invested in it.

Original_Search_Title: Need to Balance Quantity And Quality
Headline: Africa: Need to Balance Quantity And Quality
Body_Text:
AFRICA'S HUNGER PROBLEM is far deeper than the immediate lack of food. When global TV cameras shift from images of starving children with distended bellies, Africa's real challenge of malnutrition begins.
A recent study by Africa Harvest confirmed that for most Africans, access to food is more important than the quality or nature of food. No wonder 386 agricultural research activities in 26 African countries focus on basic research, such as micro-propagation to increase seedlings.
