There is, indeed, urgent need to deliver food to those who need it most.

Original_Search_Title: Storm Razes Homes in Kayunga District
Headline: Uganda: Storm Razes Homes in Kayunga District
Body_Text:
Kayunga — PROPERTY worth millions of shillings has been destroyed after a storm brought down homes and ravaged crops in Kayunga District.
The Saturday evening storm destroyed about 150 homes in Kayunga town, Kayunga Sub-county and Kangulumira township, leaving about 200 residents homeless.

Original_Search_Title: Empowering Women in the Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Empowering Women in the Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Lagos — Akwa-Ibom, Delta & Rivers states are the sites for an innovative pilot project being implemented by the United Nations Development Fund for Women (UNIFEM) in collaboration with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) and funded by the Government of Japan.
The project supports improved access of women to information for economic decision-making through increased use of ICT facilities and training. It also supports the provision of technical and entrepreneurship skills to women and provides equipment such as sewing machines, farm implements, cassava and palm oil processing equipment, palm kernel cracking equipment to the women's groups.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture for Development
Headline: Africa: Agriculture for Development
Body_Text:
Washington, DC — World Bank calls for agricultural renewal, focus on productivity growth in Sub-Saharan Africa
The latest World Development Report calls for greater investment in agriculture in Africa and warns that the sector must be placed at the center of the Region's development agenda if the goals of halving extreme poverty and hunger by 2015 are to be realized.

Original_Search_Title: Eat Well to Pass Examinations
Headline: Uganda: Eat Well to Pass Examinations
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is examination time countrywide. National examinations are underway and end-of-year exams will take off soon. Students need a certain degree of intelligence to pass the exams.
Among the factors that shape the lives of children, nutrition plays a major role. Nutrition factors affect the way our brains process information.

Original_Search_Title: Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA)
Headline: Africa: Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA)
Body_Text:
Accra — A meeting of Africa's leading cassava breeders zeroed in on actions needed to stop the rapid spread of cassava brown streak disease (CBSD). The resurgent crop disease has already caused a low-grade famine in northern Mozambique, and led farmers in Zanzibar to largely abandon cultivation of the critical food crop. Breeders from Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Malawi and Mozambique all noted the rising threat of the disease to small-scale farmers in their areas.
Cassava breeders at the meeting in Zanzibar 3-5 October 2007 said that the disease has recently spiked, for reasons that are unclear. Yet, breeders have already developed a number of disease-resistant varieties, and others are in the pipeline. Breeders noted that the problem lies in getting these varieties to farmers and that many African governments have stringent variety release rules that seriously delay getting the new varieties into farmers' fields.

Original_Search_Title: Why Britain Tightened Anti-Money Laundering Laws, By Gozney
Headline: Nigeria: Why Britain Tightened Anti-Money Laundering Laws, By Gozney
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sir Richard Gozney, the outgoing British High Commissioner to Nigeria recently paid a farewell visit to THISDAY.
At the session with THISDAY Editors, Gozney disclosed why UK took a tough stance against money launderers. He also spoke of his impressions about Nigerians. Excerpts

Original_Search_Title: Country to Get a Biofuel Plant
Headline: Uganda: Country to Get a Biofuel Plant
Body_Text:
A local investor is planning to set up a plant which will use foodstuff like maize, cassava and sugarcane to manufacture oil and gel for lighting and cooking respectively.
The investor, who currently imports the biofuels (synthetic oil and gel) from their South African-based parent company Liquifier Pty Limited, hopes to set up the plant in east and western Uganda, by the end of June next year.

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Calls for Leap in SSA Agricultural Renewal
Headline: Nigeria: World Bank Calls for Leap in SSA Agricultural Renewal
Body_Text:
The World Bank has called for greater investment in agriculture in Africa and warns that the sector must be placed in the centre of the region's development agenda if the goals of halving extreme poverty and hunger by 2015, are to be realized.
Titled 'Agriculture for Development,' the report says the need for action is especially urgent in Sub- Saharan Africa, where agricultural productivity growth has lagged behind other regions. Agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa employs 65 percent of the labor force and generates 32 percent of GDP growth.

Original_Search_Title: This Year's Idd Was Like an Oasis
Headline: Kenya: This Year's Idd Was Like an Oasis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Idd ul Fitr celebrations have provided the much needed respite from the packed political menu embellished by opinion polls and reeling from ODM Kenya presidential candidate Kalonzo Musyoka's "miracle" at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, last weekend.
The festivities broke to louder-than-normal calls by the Muadhin and as dawn broke on Saturday and as the bleating of goats being led to their slaughter filled the air in the Mombasa estates.

Original_Search_Title: Post-Harvest Losses
Headline: Uganda: Post-Harvest Losses
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRACE Ineza, 40, owns an acre of land in Kisoro district and is considered to be one of the most successful farmers in her village. But in the last season's sorghum harvest, she lost nearly half of what she reaped.
"I cut the stems, tied them up in bundles and left them to dry in the field. When I went back, most of it had been eaten by rats and birds.

Original_Search_Title: Africa Cries in the Midst of Plenty - Bekoe
Headline: Ghana: Africa Cries in the Midst of Plenty - Bekoe
Body_Text:
Professor Daniel Adzei Bekoe, Chairman of the Council of State on Tuesday expressed regret that though Africa had enough land and human resources, adequate food supply continue to be the worst of the problems hindering socio-economic development.
He said Africa needed to make technologies available to grow improved, high yielding crops to meet the food security problems of the growing populations and ensure that Africa attained self-sufficiency in food production.

Original_Search_Title: The Long Road to Delivering Food Aid
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: The Long Road to Delivering Food Aid
Body_Text:
Goma — The three World Food Programme (WFP) trucks laden with 34 tonnes of cornmeal, salt, and flour bound for 20,000 displaced civilians in Masisi, a town in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo's embattled North Kivu province, had just begun to snake up a steep mountain pass when the convoy ground to a halt - yet again.
Armed peacekeepers from the UN Mission in the Congo (MONUC) quickly fanned out across the narrow road, eyeing the dense jungle to the south and waiting tensely for an explanation for the delay.

Original_Search_Title: Bayelsa Govt to Assist Flood Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Bayelsa Govt to Assist Flood Victims
Body_Text:
The Bayelsa government has pledged to assist communities affected by flood in Ekeremor Local Government Area in the state.
The Secretary to the State Government (SSG), Dr Bolere Ketebu-Nwokeafor, made the pledge during an assessment tour of 21-communities devastated by the disaster on Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: UNBS Tightens Grip On Fake Goods
Headline: Uganda: UNBS Tightens Grip On Fake Goods
Body_Text:
Kampala — A person who imports counterfeit products is to pay three times the cost of the goods, the Uganda National Bureau of Standards' (UNBS) quality assurance manager, Deusdedit Mubangizi, has said.
"If you imported goods worth sh20m and they are found to be counterfeit, you will have to pay a penalty of sh60m," Mubangizi told reporters at a training session about the operations of the bureau at Nakawa, Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: Insecurity Hinders Intervention in Northern Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Infrastructure - Insecurity Hinders Our Intervention in N'delta - Envoy
Body_Text:
Lagos — Netherland Ambassador to Nigeria, Arie van der Wiel, has said that the level of security in the Niger Delta is the major impediment to his country's attempt to provide social amnenities in the area.
According to him, militants activities make it difficult to embark on infrastructural and different development works in the area, rather, his country gives frequent travel warnings concerning the zone where Royal Dutch Shell carry out its activities.

Original_Search_Title: Gov Gbenga Daniel Commends News Agency of Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Gov Gbenga Daniel Commends NAN
Body_Text:
Abuja — Ogun State Governor, Gbenga Daniel, said Wednesday in Abuja that the state had done well, "at least beyond average" in achieving the targets of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
Daniel made the declaration as a guest of the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture - Gov't to Collaborate With Firm
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture - Gov't to Collaborate With Firm
Body_Text:
Akure — To harness the synergy between the public and private sector in agriculture, Ondo State is collaborating with a private company, Shepherd Agriculture Nigeria Ltd, to introduce large scale commercial mecahnised farming mainly in the area of food production.
The state Governor, Olusegun Agagu, disclosed this yesterday at an event marking the World Food Day, adding that government had spent over N2 billion on agriculture in the last four years.

Original_Search_Title: Omehia to Launch Operation 100 Millionaires Next Week
Headline: Nigeria: Omehia to Launch Operation 100 Millionaires Next Week
Body_Text:
Abuja — Governor Celestine Omehia of Rivers State says his much awaited programme aimed at turning out 100 millionaires yearly among youths in the state will be unveiled next week.
He made the declaration on Tuesday at the Isaac Boro Park, Port Harcourt at a ceremony marking the 2007 World Food Day in the state. He said the school to land programme will be revitalized and that the youths will undergo training there and in other places for skills acquisition after which they will be empowered with N1 million each to start off their businesses.

Original_Search_Title: Delta - Beyond the Oil Windfall
Headline: Nigeria: Delta - Beyond the Oil Windfall
Body_Text:
Lagos — Concerns about the future relevance of oil are not new. Neither is the fact that the developed countries are bent on discovering new sources of energy for two major reasons - the unstable supply of oil (and its consequent high cost) and the environmental pollution associated with the use of petroleum products as sources of energy.
As prosperity spread round the world and the use of automobiles keep increasing, the use of petroleum products for individual and industrial needs keep increasing. The attendant pollution is worrying to the developed countries, which have discovered that they are spending billions of Dollars annually on the health bills of their people.

Original_Search_Title: Sh50b Needed to Restore Agriculture After the Floods
Headline: Uganda: Sh50b Needed to Restore Agriculture After the Floods
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE north and north-eastern region will need massive efforts if the agricultural sector is to be restored to its pre-floods level. Agriculture is the source of livelihood in this part of the country, with over 95% of the population depending on it.
However, even before the floods, the region was regarded the most food insecure in the country becuse of insurgency. The floods made the situation worse.

Original_Search_Title: Boom Time in Gulu
Headline: Uganda: Boom Time in Gulu
Body_Text:
Gulu — Two years ago Gulu was a war town. Every morning the people would wake up to the sound of hundreds of children making their way back home after a night seeking refuge from the Lord's Resistance Army in the town's shelters. In the evening, buses would race up from Kampala to make the city limits before nightfall.
But Gulu is changing fast. With peace talks progressing in Juba, capital of Southern Sudan, its air of desolation is fast being replaced by a hustle and bustle as energetic as that of any Ugandan market town. Developers are competing to get their buildings up, with a string of new constructions set to break the three-storey mark - skyscraper territory in downtown Gulu.

Original_Search_Title: Janvière Congera - 'Do They Want to Make US Refugees in Our Own Country?'
Headline: Burundi: Janvière Congera - 'Do They Want to Make US Refugees in Our Own Country?'
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — Janvière Congera, 30, was repatriated from Tanzania last July after spending four years as a refugee in Kibondo district. With her husband and five children, she chose to settle at Giharo in the southeastern province of Rutana, fearing their original home in Gitega province, in central Burundi, was still unsafe.
"Before we fled to Tanzania, we were living at Gitega; we had a small piece of land but we cannot go back there. I cannot speak for my husband but I think we won't go back. When we left, members of our family had been killed, we have nothing left there.

Original_Search_Title: Woman Charged for Child Cruelty
Headline: Sierra Leone: Woman Charged for Child Cruelty
Body_Text:
Freetown — Massah Alpha, 23 was last week charged to court by the Family Support Unit (FSU), Kenema for allegedly burning the right hand of Regina Kallon, 11.
According to Police Constable 2531 Kabbia, the suspect, residing at Saama village, Dama chiefdom, Kenema district was reported to have forcefully burnt the right hand of Kallon.

Original_Search_Title: One of the Most Favourite Tourist Spots
Headline: Seychelles: One of the Most Favourite Tourist Spots
Body_Text:
Kampala — SEYCHELLES, one of the most beautiful tropical islands in the world, is home to the love-nut, the world's largest seed. Officially known as Coco de mer, the love-nut was nick-named so because of its suggestive shape. It is a species of palm that grows only on two islands of Seychelles, Praslin and Curieuse.
It is the natural heritage of the Seychelles that make it one of the world's favourite tourist destinations. It is home to spectacular marine life and endemic plant, some of the largest seabird colonies in the world and animal species like the Seychelles giant tortoises.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security, Still a Challenge, Says FAO
Headline: Nigeria: Food Security, Still a Challenge, Says FAO
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Food and Agriculture Organisation Representative in Nigeria, Dr. Helder Muteria, said in spite of the significant agricultural growth in Nigeria, food security is still a challenge.
Muteia, who made this assertion in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria yesterday in Abuja, called for increased budgetary allocation to agriculture and research to address the situation.

Original_Search_Title: Child Labour Frustrating Education in Yumbe
Headline: Uganda: Child Labour Frustrating Education in Yumbe
Body_Text:
Yumbe — Looking at other children going to school, 12- year Rashid Asiku could not hold back his tears as his father shouted at him to go and weed the cassava field. Asiku slowly walked back into the room, changed from his school uniform, picked up a hoe and headed to the garden.
The young boy might have felt worthless and hollow as he watched playmates go to school, but Asiku is not alone. Child labour has been identified as the worst cause of high school drop outs in Yumbe District.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Biotechnology - Mixed Blessing
Headline: Zimbabwe: Agric Biotechnology - Mixed Blessing
Body_Text:
Harare — While  Zimbabwean scientists are upbeat about the need to use biotechnology to improve agricultural productivity, agronomists and environmentalists argue that emerging technologies could have serious implications on the operations of smallholder farmers in Zimbabwe and Africa as a whole.
Agronomists and environmentalists who attended a three-day workshop on the implications of Economic Partnership Agreements and the need for the protection of farmers' rights which was held last week in Harare said modern biotechnology is very expensive in terms of access, affordability and suitability for smallholder farmers in developing countries.

Original_Search_Title: Enugu Parleys South African Businessmen On Fish Production
Headline: Nigeria: Enugu Parleys South African Businessmen On Fish Production
Body_Text:
Talks have reached advanced stage between the Enugu State government and some businessmen from South Africa on fish production.
The State commissioner for agriculture and natural resource, Prof. Hilary Edeoga, made this known to newsmen in Enugu last Friday.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Airlifts Relief to North
Headline: Uganda: WFP Airlifts Relief to North
Body_Text:
Kampala — The World Food Programme (WFP) has started airlifting 3,000 metric tonnes of relief food items to 248,000 internally displaced persons in Acholi and floods victims in Lango.
The food which includes maize, beans and corn soya, is being airlifted from the UPDF military airbase in Gulu using a WFP helicopter.

Original_Search_Title: What Experts Say of the Floods Ravaging Us
Headline: Uganda: What Experts Say of the Floods Ravaging Us
Body_Text:
Kampala — Climate scientists have confirmed that the climate is actually changing. For us in Uganda, climate change is no longer a story but rather a reality as most parts of the country, especially the north and east are already feeling the impacts of heavy rains in form of floods.
Climate change is any noticeable change in the earth's climate such as a shift in seasons, increased temperature in one area, increased rains and consequent floods, storms in other regions.

Original_Search_Title: Humanitarian Agencies Receives $6m for Flood Interventions
Headline: Uganda: Humanitarian Agencies Receives $6m for Flood Interventions
Body_Text:
Kampala — HUMANITARIAN agencies operating in Uganda have received a US$ 6m (Shs10.4 bn) grant from the Central Emergency Response Fund (Cerf) for emergency relief activities in response to the flooding that hit sevaral parts of eastern and northern Uganda.
The grant is in response to a US$41 million Flash Appeal launched by the relief agencies last month.

Original_Search_Title: 200 Children Rescued from Pastors
Headline: Uganda: 200 Children Rescued From Gulu Pastors
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE POLICE on Friday stormed a new church for born-again Christians in Gulu district and rescued over 200 children who were under illegal custody.
The children have been reunited with their parents.

Original_Search_Title: Forget Helicopters; Instead Start Rearing Horses And Asses
Headline: Uganda: Forget Helicopters; Instead Start Rearing Horses And Asses
Body_Text:
Although the rains are still falling and the floods still bothering our people in different parts of Uganda, time to stop blaming anybody has come. What is needed is to assess our capability to deal with such disasters and move on to lay a foundation upon which the ministry responsible for disaster preparedness can base its future operations.
Today's disaster preparedness is nothing to write home about and there is no point crucifying the gentlemen who run the shambolic ministry. After all, how many helicopters are there in this country for instance? Two and a half or three.

Original_Search_Title: Mombasa Gets a Rare Makeover
Headline: Kenya: Mombasa Gets a Rare Makeover
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The surrounding caves, which served as holding grounds for slaves waiting to be shipped to the Middle East and other destinations more than 400 years ago, were used to commit some of the vices.
Gangs used caves as hideouts to pounce on unsuspecting visitors.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Minister Warns West On Impending Heavy Rains
Headline: Uganda: Agriculture Minister Warns West On Impending Heavy Rains
Body_Text:
Mbarara — THE State Minister for Agriculture, Dr Kibirige Ssebunya, has appealed to farmers in the south western region to prepare for impending heavy rains that are likely to cause floods and landslides.
In a letter to district production departments, Dr Ssebunya said forecasts show the region will get heavy rains which may be above normal starting mid October and lasting up to December. He says these rains will affect crop and livestock production and hence poor incomes and low food security.

Original_Search_Title: Niger Economy, the PPP And Vision 2020
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Economy, the PPP And Vision 2020
Body_Text:
Minna — At any rate, Niger state epitomises the economic backwardness in the northern part of Nigeria where majority of its people live below the stipulated $1 dollar a day. Yet successive governments have made frantic efforts to break the yoke of ravaging poverty with little success recorded.
Critics are of the view that little achievements have been recorded in the first 100 days of the new government. They accused the governor of talking much and doing less in terms of redeeming his pledges to turn the state around.

Original_Search_Title: Independence in France with Juliana
Headline: Uganda: Independence in France with Juliana
Body_Text:
Like any other city, Paris is full of life. The witty conmen who sell fake merchandise, creepy characters that give suspicious glares and tricksters who do everything in their means to unwillingly relieve you of your possessions.
You could know it all, but unlike in other cities like Nairobi , you get tempted to walk on the lonely streets simply because of the allure of Paris.

Original_Search_Title: Bio Fuel - Proda Needs N1b for Upgrading
Headline: Nigeria: Bio Fuel - Proda Needs N1b for Upgrading
Body_Text:
The Project Development Institute {PRODA} has produced a bio fuel from ethanol for the movement of automobiles and other heavy machines in the country
Director general of the institute, Prof Goddy Nkem Onuoha , who made this known yesterday while conducting newsmen round the facilities at the headquarters of the institute in Enugu, disclosed that the PRODA requires about N1 billion for the upgrading of its facilities .

Original_Search_Title: Use of Cassava for Ethanol Production Defended
Headline: Mozambique: Use of Cassava for Ethanol Production Defended
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican scientists and researchers told an International Symposium on Tropical Roots and Tubers that they are determined to develop varieties of cassava appropriate for the production of bio-fuels.
They were speaking in Maputo on Wednesday, the third day of the meeting, on the theme "Roots and Tubers for the production of bio-fuels: Challenges and Opportunities".

Original_Search_Title: Beyond Oil And Gas - Options for Development of a Robust Ijaw
Headline: Nigeria: Beyond Oil And Gas - Options for Development of a Robust Ijaw
Body_Text:
Abuja — Decades ago, when Isaac boro, the legendary Ijaw patriot took up arms to liberate his people, he envisaged a future in which the Ijaws would be in control of not just their political but also their economic destiny. His rebellion failed and four decades later, the Ijaws and other people of the Niger Delta, have not made economic progress commensurate with their resources and contributions to the Nigerian nation. Oil and gas from the Niger Delta have built brand new cities like Abuja , produced Nigerians who will be billionaires in any currency and transformed the economic landscape of the country. However, Ijaw land has, in some parts, remained much what it was in the 60's, so much that most parts of the Ijaw land are accessible only by water. Apart from the appropriation of the petroleum resources of the Ijaw land by the Nigerian nation, the people have enough resources to transform themselves into a formidable economic group. Besides, the tragedy of Nigeria 's economic development is that no one thinks of the "oil box".
With gratitude to Mother Nature, Ijaw land is well endowed by a mix of natural resources and an enterprising population. The Niger Delta, home to the Ijaws, is a treasure trough. As the world's third largest wetland, it is also an area of scenic beauty. It has a beautiful coastal line and a network of rivers and creeks. It is pointless mentioning that oil was first drilled from Oloibiri in Bayelsa State, in 1958.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture to Aid Flood-Hit Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Agriculture to Aid Flood-Hit Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Ministry of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries has earmarked Shs4.8 billion to enable flood-hit farmers in the north and eastern regions resume food production as soon as the floods recede.
"The money will be used, among other things, to acquire high yielding and fast growing crops for staple food like cassava stems, potato vines, cow peas, and upland rice," said Mr Okaasai Opolot, the commissioner for crop production and marketing.

Original_Search_Title: Another Boy Killed in Bomb Blast
Headline: Uganda: Another Boy Killed in Bomb Blast
Body_Text:
Kampala — A bomb exploded yesterday in Mbogwe village Buhanika sub-county in Hoima district, killing a 13-year-old boy. The second victim, a sister to the deceased, was rushed to Hoima Hospital in critical condition following the incident at Buhanika, about 10kms from Hoima town.
The officer in charge of crime at Hoima Police Station, John Bahemuka, identified the deceased as Bernard Atuhairwe, a Primary Five pupil at St. Petero Paul Primary School in Buhanika. His sister, Angella Nsungwa, 15, is a Primary Six pupil in the same school.

Original_Search_Title: 'Five Million Citizens Malnourished'
Headline: Uganda: 'Five Million Citizens Malnourished'
Body_Text:
Kampala — ABOUT five million people in Uganda are malnourished, according to the agriculture ministry. Okaasi Opolot, the commissioner for production and marketing, said most of them live in the rural areas.
About 7.75 million in the countryside face food insecurity, he said.

Original_Search_Title: FG Told to be Firm on Cassava Initiative
Headline: Nigeria: FG Told to be Firm on Cassava Initiative
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has been told to be firm in the implementation of the presidential initiative on cassava. This is with a view to ensuring that flour millers in the country make use of five per cent cassava content in their products as stipulated in the initiative.
The Chairman of All Farmers Association of Nigeria (AFAN), Dr. Tunde Arosanyin, made the call in Lokoja, in an interview. He traced the glut that characterised the production of cassava in many states of the federation to a lack of effective implementation of the objectives of the policy. According to him, the absence of enforcement on the flour millers to comply with the mandate as contained in the initiative has brought a negative effect on cassava processing plants.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Renders 4 Communities Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Renders 4 Communities Homeless
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — No less than 2,700 people in four communities of Ikwo Local Government Area, Ebonyi State have been rendered homeless by a rampaging flood which also destroyed crops including rice and yam worth about N100 million.
The overflow of a river in Cross River State after torrential rain caused the flood, which swept the border communities of Ndiagu Amagu, Ndiagu Igbudu, Ndiagu Inyimagu and Ndiagu Achara in the Ikwo council area, home of the state governor, Chief Martin Elechi.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Hit Bushenyi, Destroy Crops
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Hit Bushenyi, Destroy Crops
Body_Text:
Bushenyi — RESIDENTS of Buhweju County in Bushenyi District have appealed to the government for relief assistance after heavy rains destroyed their homes and gardens.
The rains that fell on Saturday lasted several hours, destroying banana, cassava, peas and bean gardens and the roofs of schools and homes in Bihanga Sub-county.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Displaces 3,000
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Sacks 3,000 in Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Not less than 3000 inhabitants in four boarder communities of Ikwo local government area, Ebonyi State have been sacked by surging flood from the banks of Cross River that demarcates Ebonyi from Cross River State.
Consequently, food crops estimated at over N100 million were equally lost to the torrent that overflowed the communities of Ndiagu-Amagu, Ndiagu-Igbudu, Ndiagu-Inyimagu and Ndiagu-Achara, all in the Ikwo, home council area of the Ebonyi State Governor, Chief Martin Elechi.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Renders 3000 Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Renders 3000 Homeless
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — No fewer than 3000 persons, most of them widows and indigent children were yesterday rendered homeless, while properties, including food crops said to be worth over N100 million were totally destroyed, following an early morning flood that swept through four communities in Ikwo Local Government area of Ebonyi State.
The torrent, according to eyewitness account, was an over flow from the bank of a river in Cross River State that swept Ndiagu Amagu, Ndiagu Igbudu, Ndiagu Inyimagu and Ndiagu Achara, which indidentally is the home council of Governor Martin Elechi.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Destroy Four Mbarara Schools
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Destroy Four Mbarara Schools
Body_Text:
Kampala — HEAVY rains on Saturday hit Mwizi sub-county in Mbarara district damaging four schools, 30 houses, a church, parish offices and crops. The sub-county chairman, Vanansi Rurema, said the schools were St. Jude's Bushweere, Kikunda and Kyonyo primary schools and Bushweere United Secondary School.
At St. Judes, six classrooms and a library were damaged. The roofs of some teachers' quarters, school kitchen and store were blown off, he said.

Original_Search_Title: Scientific Research Must Guarantee Food Security
Headline: Mozambique: Scientific Research Must Guarantee Food Security
Body_Text:
Maputo — Mozambican Agriculture Minister Erasmo Muhate said in Maputo on Monday that scientific research must contribute to guaranteeing the food security of subsistence farmers.
He was speaking during the opening session of an International Symposium on the Cultivation of Tropical Roots and Tubers, in Maputo, attended by scientists from several African countries.

Original_Search_Title: Osunbor Decries Neglect of Cassava Plant
Headline: Nigeria: Osunbor Decries Neglect of Cassava Plant
Body_Text:
Benin — Edo State governor, Professor Oserheimen Osunbor has decried the state of neglect of the ultra modern Cassavita plant at Uromi, saying that it was a tragedy that the state-owned processing factory, was being allowed to remain domant after attaining 90 per cent completion.
It would be recalled that the administration of former Governor Lucky Igbinedion, had initiated and completed the factory as part of its industrialization programme.

Original_Search_Title: President Opens Aids Drugs Factory
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Opens Aids Drugs Factory
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has said some people within the Government tried to sabotage the construction of Africa's first plant to manufacture the full triple therapy combination of anti-retroviral medicines.
"I supported the construction of this plant immediately because we have a big number of our people with malaria and AIDS," the President said yesterday at the inauguration of the $30m hi-tech pharmaceutical factory in Luzira that will also manufacture anti-malarial drugs.

Original_Search_Title: The Lawyer as Brand Consultant
Headline: Nigeria: The Lawyer as Brand Consultant
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Alder Group, consisting Alder Consulting, Alder B&M, Alder IT and Alder Research is well known in the country today.
Amongst its well known and sometimes controversial programmes are: mind the_gap, as well as the Heart of Africa project. Alder was also behind the successful Orange Rules campaign of GT Bank recently. But not many know that the founder of this policy and strategy firm, Leke Alder, is also a lawyer. FUNKE ABOYADE spent an evening with him recently, to find out just what makes this extraordinary man of ideas tick

Original_Search_Title: Hailstorm Destroys Masaka Plantations
Headline: Uganda: Hailstorm Destroys Masaka Plantations
Body_Text:
Kampala — ABOUT 250 households in Bukoto mid-west in Masaka district were left without food after a hailstorm destroyed their plantations. The affected villages were Kyassenyi, Nakalinzi, Bwangannyondo and Kawundiro in Lwengo sub-counties. The September 29 storm also hit Ndagwe sub-county.
Vincent Kasule, the LCI chairman for Kyassenya village, said most banana, cassava, sweet potato, beans and maize gardens were destroyed.

Original_Search_Title: Ohakim And Challenge of Governance
Headline: Nigeria: Ohakim And Challenge of Governance
Body_Text:
Lagos — Early months of any administration provide an ample time for the public to form impressions of that administration. Such impressions are natural in the process of governance. Impressions are fluid, they never remain the same. And in politics, impressions assume a far reaching implication. This is so because it enables doubts or confirm them. A lot of things happen in politics and we cannot but say, "God moves in mysterious ways". It's a maxim we ponder about without actually assimilating its full import. The mysteries of God cannot be understood by mortal man. God knows our needs more than we do. We pontificate on those too, but when things turn out contrary to our expectations, we grieve. We lament and wonder why our carefully laid plans did not work out. We often forget that maybe, just maybe God wants to do something better than that which we had planned.
When the governorship election in Imo state went contrary to expectations the people wondered aloud. There was confusion all over the place. People were perplexed, not knowing what to expect. The greatest worry was whether the Anambra example was now to play out in Imo. What else could the populace think when Chief Ikedi Godson Ohakim of the Peoples Progressive Alliance (PPA) was declared Governor-elect in a state whose House of Assembly was dominated by the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). The worst-case scenario haunted the poor masses and when considered against the backdrop of the myriad of problems that preceded the elections in the state then such fears expressed by the people would be understood.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Opportunism
Headline: Nigeria: Economic Opportunism
Body_Text:
Days of sharp and long knives are here. So, watch your sides, your front, your back and even the air above you, as well as the very ground on which you walk. No one is safe anymore from anyone. Nigerians, by and large, worship at the altar of Mammon and do go on to taunt God with their raucous religious hypocrisy and this is precisely why our economic mess compounds itself by the day.
For, if the millions of Nigerians who today claim to be Christians and Muslims were sincerely true and faithful to their confessions, would there not have emerged in Nigeria the critical mass of staunch and ecumenical people of God to bring down, through fasting and prayers, the justice and peace of God upon the whole land so that we may become citizens all and subjects none in Nigeria, this amazingly endowed heartbeat of the black race? There surely would. But here we are, full of deceitful and heartless talk and hardly ever loyal to any act of intellectual or spiritual faith, because we are opportunism writ large; and this is why we are so loudly as empty as empty can be.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't Authorizes Rice Price Increment, Sortage Imminent
Headline: Liberia: Gov't Authorizes Rice Price Increment, Sortage Imminent
Body_Text:
A few month ago, the Ministry of Commerce informed the public that there were six importers of rice in Liberia, hence there will be no shortage.
But recent investigation conducted by this paper revealed that stocks at the Freeport of Monrovia have dropped to 16, 000 metric tons and some importers have expressed fear of looming rice shortage.

Original_Search_Title: Kuje Council Distributes Palm Seedlings
Headline: Nigeria: Kuje Council Distributes Palm Seedlings
Body_Text:
Kuje Area Council of FCT has distributed more than 1,000 palm seedlings to boost production.
The supervisory councillor for Agriculture, Mr Samson Barde, made this knownWednesday in Kuje in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).

Original_Search_Title: UN Launches Flash Appeal to Aid 75,000 Flood Victims
Headline: Ghana: UN Launches Flash Appeal to Aid 75,000 Flood Victims
Body_Text:
The United Nations and partner organizations today launched a $10-million flash appeal for 75,000 people in northern Ghana, where entire communities have been hit by floods after heavy and persistent rain in late August and mid-September.
Farmers in the already vulnerable Northern, Upper East and Upper West Regions have lost their crops, vast tracts of land, food storage and processing facilities are submerged, and houses, bridges, schools and health facilities have been destroyed by the waters, which are part of a wider flood crisis across a whole swath of sub-Saharan Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Airtime Tops Spending List
Headline: Uganda: Airtime Tops Spending List
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS generally spent more on telephone airtime in September than they did on food items, a survey by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics has shown.
According to the Consumer Price Index (CPI) released by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (Ubos) on Tuesday, the highest expenditure for most people was on telecommunications services.

Original_Search_Title: Omehia Pledges Support for Small Scale Businesses
Headline: Nigeria: Omehia Pledges Support for Small Scale Businesses
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — Governor Celestine Omehia of Rivers State has said that every Rivers person wishing to embark on small or medium scale business "will be empowered through direct funding or by laying solid foundation for the people to be economically self-reliant."
Accordingly, he said the eradication of poverty will be given adequate attention because the "objective of the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) is in synergy with that of the state government."

Original_Search_Title: Kuje Area Council Distributes Palm Seedlings to Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Kuje Area Council Distributes Palm Seedlings to Farmers
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Kuje Area Council of the FCT has distributed more than 1,000 palm seedlings to boost production.
The Supervisory Councillor for Agriculture, Mr Samson Barde, made this known yesterday in Kuje in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN).

Original_Search_Title: Districts Not Prepared for Disasters
Headline: Uganda: Districts Not Prepared for Disasters
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE current floods in northern Uganda have brought into question the ability of districts to handle disasters. Local leaders in the affected districts are wondering about what they can do to help the population. Julius Ochen, the LC5 chairman of Amuria district, says they need about sh50b to help the flood victims.
"This is a disaster," says Kaberamaido LC5 chairman, Robert Engulu.

Original_Search_Title: Gbagyi Farmers Urged to Adopt New Technique
Headline: Nigeria: Gbagyi Farmers Urged to Adopt New Technique
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Gbagyi communties in the Federal-Capital Territory (FCT) and neighbouring states have been advised to adopt new approach to farming for economic empowerment.
Speaking during a one-day seminar and peace music concert for enlightenment dialogue entitled "Empowering the Masses for Agricultural Revolution" the chairman of the occasion, Alhaji Abubakar Wodi advised Gbagyi farmers to organize themselves into cooperatives or enterprise groups and adopt new approach to production, processing and marketing.

Original_Search_Title: These Names On Plantains
Headline: Cameroon: These Names On Plantains
Body_Text:
If you say plantains are not the most coveted of food items these days (especially in the southern parts of Cameroon); then you've got it wrong.
A sizeable bunch can't cost less than 2,000 francs. And as wholesalers from other CEMAC countries flood into our local markets for plantains in particular, our economists tell us demand outweighs supply. Simply put, those involved in plantain farming are therefore in lucrative business.

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuffs Prices Increase Countrywide
Headline: Rwanda: Foodstuffs Prices Increase Countrywide
Body_Text:
Kigali — Climatic change and external factors are to blame for the current increase in prices of foodstuffs in the country, a Central bank official has said.
A mini survey of markets and shops in the city indicates that prices of some foodstuffs and other essential commodities have continued to increase, some by as much as 50 percent, the highest in the country in past three months.

Original_Search_Title: Ministry Spearheads Projects for Women
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ministry Spearheads Projects for Women
Body_Text:
Harare — Government is committed to improving the standards of living for women in Zimbabwe through self-help projects to fight drought-induced hunger and poverty in rural areas, the Minister of Women's Affairs, Gender and Community Development, Cde Oppah Muchinguri, has said.
She said her ministry was in the process of establishing self-help projects at the grassroots level to cater for marginalised women.

Original_Search_Title: SOS From Odoni Kingdom
Headline: Nigeria: SOS From Odoni Kingdom
Body_Text:
THE lack of a motorable road and the alarming insecurity on the waterways have become a source of serious concern to the people of Odoni Kingdom. However, my first year of reign as the Pere of Odoni Kingdom has been quite eventful. One of the notable activities which took place is the signing of a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with the Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) at Yenagoa. This was after some Odoni indigenes were trained for this purpose. In addition to this, construction work at a World Bank-assisted cottage hospital through the Bayelsa State Ministry of Health has commenced.
Also significant was the awareness campaign against the dreaded HIV/AIDS. Another remarkable and significant event in the first year of my reign was the peaceful elections which led to the emergence of a democratically-elected civilian Government headed by President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua with His Excellency, Goodluck Jonathan as Vice-President on 29th May, 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Brazilian Investors Plan Mega Cities for Some States in Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Brazilian Investors Plan Mega Cities for Some States in Nigeria
Body_Text:
Lagos — A consortium of Brazilian investors that is interested in building mega cities in some states in Nigeria is expected in the country next week to seek land for the project.
The delegation, which will be led by the Governor of Central Bank of Brazil, would meet with some state governors, especially those with whom they had earlier commenced discussions in Brazil. The states governors include those of Lagos, Ogun, Imo, Abia, Gombe, Delta and Osun states.

Original_Search_Title: Workshop to Celebrate Country's Achievements
Headline: Zimbabwe: Workshop to Celebrate Country's Achievements
Body_Text:
Harare — THE National Biotechnology Authority will this week hold a one-day workshop to celebrate Zimbabwe's achievements, capabilities and talents in the field of biotechnology.
NBA registrar and acting chief executive officer Mr Abisai Mafa said the high profile workshop will be held on Thursday and is aimed at celebrating and promoting biotechnology research, development, application and management.

Original_Search_Title: Country at 47
Headline: Nigeria: Country at 47
Body_Text:
Lagos — AS Nigeria celebrates its 47 years of Independence tomorrow, eminent Nigerians have blamed the pace of the country's economic development on the absence of patriotism among the people and lack of commitment to the Nigerian project.
Among the eminent Nigerians who spoke on the Nigeria's 47 years of nationhood are former Petroleum Resources Minister, Senator Jibirl Aminu, former president of Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), Comrade Pascal Bafyau, Secretary-General of the Nigeria Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs (NSCIA), Alhaji Abdulateef Adegbite, former Attorney-General and justice Minister, Prince Bola Ajibola and an Ogun State-based Islamic leader, Alhaji Munirudeen Nusirudeen.

Original_Search_Title: Freedom Fight Hideout Cries for Recognition And Protection
Headline: Kenya: Freedom Fight Hideout Cries for Recognition And Protection
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is one of the numerous breath-taking sites on the picturesque Taita Hills. Nestling on the foot of one of the hills, the Kenyatta Cave, known to the locals as the Kino cave, is at the tiny Mwanguwi village, about 6km from Wundanyi, the headquarters of the larger Taita-Taveta.
The villagers talk excitedly about the ancient cave as they recall and savour its long and eventful history. It is believed to have been one of the hideouts of Mzee Jomo Kenyatta at the height of the struggle for independence.

Original_Search_Title: Brothers Held Over Rape of Stepmother
Headline: Uganda: Brothers Held Over Rape of Stepmother
Body_Text:
Kampala — TWO brothers in Atiak internally displaced persons' cam in Amuru district have been arrested for raping their 60-year old stepmother, the Police has said.
On September 15, Joseph Okumu, 21 and James Akena, 34, followed their stepmother to the field, where they raped her, the community liaison officer, Robinal Oceng, told journalists.

Original_Search_Title: Gulu's Brighter Side
Headline: Uganda: Gulu's Brighter Side
Body_Text:
Kampala — I WILL never be the same again. That is one thing I know for sure. I have grown up hearing about nothing else about this region but the war and it had never occurred to me that I could travel to northern Uganda.
Its not that I didn't feel for these innocent people who have suffered the war for two decades.

Original_Search_Title: Plant Oil for Vehicles
Headline: Namibia: Plant Oil for Vehicles
Body_Text:
Windhoek — International University of Management - Namibia Oil contributes immensely to the human advancement. Furthermore, oil keeps our automobiles, trains, ships, airlines and entire civilization on the move.
However, the world's oil wells are going to dry within a few decades. Meanwhile, new economic giants -- Brazil, China, India, wealthy America, Japan, and some European countries - are prepared to buy oil at any price.

Original_Search_Title: Alliance for a Green Revolution - a Blunt Philanthropic Arrow
Headline: Africa: Alliance for a Green Revolution - a Blunt Philanthropic Arrow
Body_Text:
Rather than proposing techno-fixes to problems of agricultural development in Africa, donors could better assist in the development of rural infrastructure such as roads and water supplies, and education to empower the younger generation in the study of useful science. African farmers, along with peasants around the world, are seeking respect for their right to decide on what to plant and how to plant it, as well what to eat and how.
It is a common saying that when a man has a hammer in his hand every problem appears to be a nail. It takes a wise man to know that a hammer is just one of the tools in the craftsman's box. The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation made money from technology. It is understandable that they should think that problems can always be solved with a technological fix. Nor is it surprising that the Rockefeller and Gates Foundations should plan to jointly plough $150,000,000 into their so-called Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA). Tragically, the biotech solutions proposed by AGRA are likely to deepen rather than solve problems of hunger, poverty and malnutrition in Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Big Potential And Challenges for Biofuels
Headline: Africa: Big Potential And Challenges for Biofuels
Body_Text:
Durban — Biofuels offer Africa the chance to supply itself with alternative energy sources, and also to become a major supplier of these sources for developed markets. Yet, challenges -- from creating the relevant infrastructure to competition for biofuel crops from food markets -- remain.
"There is huge room for exploitation of clean sources of energy at a time when the entire world is confronted with global warming," said Salvador Namburete, Mozambique's minister of energy.

Original_Search_Title: They Raise School Fees By Roasting Maize
Headline: Uganda: They Raise School Fees By Roasting Maize
Body_Text:
Kampala — ROSEMARY Nansambu, 9, would not be studying at Shalom Primary School, Mulago, if she was not selling maize on the streets.
She began selling maize last year after the death of her father, Charles Muyondo. Nansambu cannot recall the name of her mother, who died when she was younger but says: "Whenever I think of my mother, I cry."

Original_Search_Title: Teso Floods Damage Estimated at Shs120b
Headline: Uganda: Teso Floods Damage Estimated at Shs120b
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE cost of the damage caused by floods in Teso sub-region has been put at Shs120 billion. The figure covers six districts of Amuria, Katakwi, Bukedea, Kaberamaido, Kumi and Soroti.
According to a report compiled by LC5 chairpersons in the areas, Amuria District requires Shs50.8 billion for reconstruction while Bukedea needs Shs9.6 billion, Kaberamaido Shs4.3 billion, Katakwi Shs25.3 billion, Kumi Shs12.1 billion and Soroti Shs17.6 billion. Most of the funds would go towards restocking food, relocation of IDP camps, rehabilitation and improvement of school environment, reconstruction of roads, disease out breaks, shelter and safe water among others. The region local leaders are appealing for national and international intervention.

Original_Search_Title: Widows Cashing in On Garbage
Headline: Rwanda: Widows Cashing in On Garbage
Body_Text:
Kigali — Buoyed by the increasing volume of garbage in Kigali City, the Dusabane Association is cashing in on making and selling manure from the rubbish.
Each home is charged at least Frw1, 000 monthly in order to keep Kigali clean from generating too much garbage.

Original_Search_Title: Projects to Resettle IDPs Set Up
Headline: Uganda: Projects to Resettle IDPs Set Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — They clapped and chanted as their group leader received oxen and ox-ploughs.
"Don't use them here in the camp. Go and use it in your village," said Jimmy Owani of the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO), who was handing over the implements at Unyama in Gulu.

Original_Search_Title: Sunflower - New Hope for Bukedea
Headline: Uganda: Sunflower - New Hope for Bukedea
Body_Text:
Kampala — While most crops in Teso are rotting in the fields due to floods, a farmers group in Malera sub-county in Bukedea District hopes to bag sh441m from sunflower.
Michael Ikara, the deputy chairperson of the Popular Kumi Women's Initiative (PAKWI), a grassroots group says: "Unlike most crops, the sunflower can be grown on the same piece of land four times in a year. The crop is neither affected by heavy rains nor prolonged drought.Besides, it takes only two days to dry after harvest.

Original_Search_Title: Food Production to Halve By 2020
Headline: Africa: Food Production to Halve By 2020
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Food security in Africa is likely to be "severely compromised" by climate change, with production expected to halve by 2020, according to climate change experts.
The projections are contained in a report launched last week in London by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which was followed by an experts' panel discussion.

Original_Search_Title: LWF/WS, Finn Church Aid Assist Farmers
Headline: Liberia: LWF/WS, Finn Church Aid Assist Farmers
Body_Text:
The Lutheran World Federation/Department of World Service (LWF/WS) with support from an International Church Based Organization in Finland (FINN CHURCH AID), has empowered thousands of citizens and residents of Panta and Zota Districts in Bong County in the areas of Agriculture, and the construction of bridges, hand pumps, latrines, among others.
The citizens and residents, who have been speaking to the press in Bong County, praised the Lutheran World Federation/World Service and the FINN CHURCH AID for empowering them through the various productive activities, which now serve as a source of livelihood.

Original_Search_Title: The Battle of the Floods
Headline: Uganda: The Battle of the Floods
Body_Text:
Amaserniko — Francis Egoliam believed the downpour was a one-off in this north-eastern region of Uganda that is usually very dry. But then waves of water started swirling through his homestead and the houses of unbaked mud bricks started melting, leaving his family homeless and hungry.
"We all thought it was going to be short-lived but we realised it had become a daily phenomenon and the floods started. Our crops have been destroyed by the water and houses have collapsed," says Egoliam of his village's ordeal at Amaseniko camp in Amuria district.

Original_Search_Title: Ethanol Production Next Year
Headline: Ghana: Ethanol Production Next Year
Body_Text:
Accra — Caltech Ventures Ghana Limited could begin the production of ethanol from cassava at Hodzo near Ho next year when its 6.5 million dollars production plant would be ready.
Managing Director, Mr. Chris Quarshie, told newsmen on Saturday that the company's total investment in the venture is 10 million U.S. dollars. The company has put 400 acres under cassava cultivation with plans to expand to 1,200 acres next year. He said 60 percent of the 6 million litres of ethanol to be produced yearly would be exported.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Transport Fares Soar
Headline: Uganda: Teso Transport Fares Soar
Body_Text:
Kampala — TRANSPORT fares and commodity prices have gone up in most parts of flood-hit Teso. The chairman of the Teso Region District Chairpersons Forum, Robert Ekongot, said moving from the sub-counties to the districts' towns costs over sh15,000 from between sh2,000 and sh3,000.
Ekongot said a sachet of salt costs sh1,000 from sh200. Earlier on, the Teso chairpersons met the Teso Parliamentary Group to discuss the effect of floods in the area.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture - Thailand Partners Investors
Headline: Nigeria: Agriculture - Thailand Partners Investors
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Thai Charge d'affairs, Mr Niyom Wattammawut has urged Nigerian businessmen to explore the many investment opportunities, especially in agriculture, available in Thailand which is the fastest growing economy in South East Asia.
This was at a seminar themed "Thai-Nigerian Partnership in Globalization" aimed at showcasing the economic potentials which Thailand holds for intending investors.

Original_Search_Title: Govt, Foreign Farmer Plan N2.6bn Farm
Headline: Nigeria: Govt, Foreign Farmer Plan N2.6bn Farm
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ondo State government and a Nigeria-based South African agricultural company, have concluded arrangements to spend N2.6 billion to establish mechanised farms in the state.
The partnership is part of the state government's efforts to boost food production, revenue generation and create abundant job opportunities.

Original_Search_Title: Osunbor On Scale
Headline: Nigeria: Osunbor On Scale
Body_Text:
Lagos — SINCE the live appearance of Senator Oserhiemen Osunbor, governor of Edo State, during the September 5 celebration of the first hundred days on television, his image has soared widely. The event has afforded the former Senate Committee chairman on Judiciary, Law and Justice, a rare opportunity to display a great oratorical skill which pundits believe was strong enough to rubbish many town criers.
Osunbor from a dwindling image has risen to become the rave of the state. And no doubt many reactions so far has given the lawyer turned politician a nod of approval which is considered as a great punch on the otherwise strong camp of the opposition led by Adams Oshiomhole of Action Congress, AC.

Original_Search_Title: Former Street Children Out to Change Life in the City Slums
Headline: Kenya: Former Street Children Out to Change Life in the City Slums
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With the election season under way, Kenyans are daily subjected to an endless succession of photo ops, press conferences and sound bytes. With all the sound and fury, it's easy to forget what any of these men are actually doing for the people they claim to represent.
Emmanuel Boys Centre founder Daniel Muiruri Nduati with Aids orphans Samuel Kamau, 9, and Kevin Kinyanjui, 14. Photos/ARNO KOPECKY

Original_Search_Title: City Food Prices to Increase
Headline: Uganda: City Food Prices to Increase
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOOD prices are likely to go up as famine takes root in flooded eastern Uganda. The situation is likely to get worse even in Kampala, as the floods cripple one of Uganda's biggest food baskets.
Kevin Ocen, a Kampala groundnut dealer who normally gets his produce from Soroti, says his supply routes have now been cut off. Not only have the roads become impassable, but the crops are also rotting in the floods.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Imminent as Crops Rot
Headline: Uganda: Famine Imminent as Crops Rot
Body_Text:
Kampala — Many former IDPs have returned to camps after their houses collapsed
Please leave that cassava for us. Don't buy it because we want it seriously. We have been starving here for the past 24 hours.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Loses $5 Billion Annually On Cassava Export
Headline: Nigeria Loses $5 Billion Annually On Cassava Export
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Flour Millers in the country yesterday received knocks for frustrating federal government's move to generate $5 billion annually from foreign revenue.
The attack came from a professor of agricultural economics, Prof. Dupe Olatunbosun, who argued that the whooping sum would have been generated through exports of cassava derivatives and import savings on wheat.

Original_Search_Title: Flour Millers Accused of Frustrating Cassava Production
Headline: Nigeria: Flour Millers Accused of Frustrating Cassava Production
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — The Chairman of the Ogun State Cassava Revolution Programme, Prof. Dupe Olatunbosun has raised alarm over the efforts by flour millers in the country to frustrate cassava producers and processors by giving excuses to reject the cassava flour in producing flour.
The development came, even as some of the millers have been accused of stock piling their warehouses to deceive the Standard Organization of Nigeria (SON), the regulatory body checking the compliance of the millers on the use of the 5% cassava flour in their production.

Original_Search_Title: Osei-Wusu Foundation Grants Loans to Farmers
Headline: Ghana: Osei-Wusu Foundation Grants Loans to Farmers
Body_Text:
Bekwai-Ash — The Osei-Owusu Foundation for Indigenous Business Development, a micro-financing service has granted ¢54 million (GH¢5,400) as loans to 54 members of a local Plantain, Cassava and Cocoyam Growers Association at Koniyaw, a farming community in the Amansie East district of the Ashanti Region.
Each farmer received an initial amount of ¢1,000,000 (GH¢100) to enable them procure plantain suckers for cultivation during this farming season and thus produce plantain and other foodstuff in large quantities.

Original_Search_Title: Country Gets $847 Million U.S. Grant to Implement Needs
Headline: Nigeria: Country Gets $847 Million U.S. Grant to Implement Needs
Body_Text:
THE United States Agency for International Development {USAID} disclosed Tuesday that of the total $845 Million it promised to support Nigeria's development till 2009, with Niger Delta as a major focus, it had granted $477 Million to the Nigerian government.
Speaking during the signing ceremony of the Amendment to the Strategic Objectives Agreement Grant between the USAID and the National Planning Commission {NPC}, Ms Sharon Cromer, USAID Mission Director noted that the American government through the agency was committed basically to prevent 1.1 million new AIV/AIDS infections, provide care and support to 1.7 million HIV affected individuals and to assist more than 400,000 orphans as well as other vulnerable children in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Alliance Funds Next Cohort of Crop Breeders
Headline: Malawi: Alliance Funds Next Cohort of Crop Breeders
Body_Text:
The Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA) is to fund a new initiative to train the next generation of African crop scientists, aiming to improve agricultural productivity and food security in the region.
The funding, which will allow African PhD students to study staple African crops, was announced today (19 September).

Original_Search_Title: President Declares State of Emergency
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Declares State of Emergency
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni is to declare a state of emergency in northern and eastern Uganda, where floods have displaced thousands, destroyed crops and property worth billions and washed away bridges and roads, cutting off entire sub-counties and districts.
During a Cabinet meeting yesterday morning, chaired by the President, it was agreed that the floods constituted a natural disaster and a state of emergency be declared, Prime Minister Apolo Nsibambi told Parliament yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Trekking Towards a People's Theatre
Headline: Kenya: Trekking Towards a People's Theatre
Body_Text:
Who said theatre business is all fun without funds in return? Ask Eliud Abuto and he will tell you that theatre is as good business as any other.
Three years ago, the Festival of Creative Arts- a local theatre company he runs in the city-rose from the ashes of the now defunct Mbalamwezi Theatre Society.

Original_Search_Title: 'Presidential Initiatives Are Economic Windows for Growth'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Presidential Initiatives Are Economic Windows for Growth'
Body_Text:
The agriculture-related initiatives of the immediate past administration, if well implemented, are windows for steady economic growth, a commodity association president has said.
Mr Mohammed Magaji, the national president of the Cassava Producers, Promoters and Exporters Association of Nigeria (CASPEAN) made this known in an interview with newsmen yesterday in Abuja.

Original_Search_Title: Teso Leaders Speak On Govt Response to the Raging Floods
Headline: Uganda: Teso Leaders Speak On Govt Response to the Raging Floods
Body_Text:
Flooding in many parts of Eastern Uganda, especially Teso have displaced thousands of people and destroyed property. How well has the government responded to the disaster? Agness Nandutu spoke to State Minister for Relief and Disaster Preparedness Mr Musa Ecweru and Soroti Woman MP Ms Alice Alaso.
As a leader in the region, how do you rate government's handling of this disaster?

Original_Search_Title: Country Marks Science Week
Headline: Uganda: Country Marks Science Week
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Vice-President, Prof Gilbert Bukenya, and many other government officials and politicians joined in the Science Walk on Monday with a call for innovation. Many of the solutions to problems brought about by changes in the environmental lie in the knowledge of some of these scientists who marched in Kampala.
With about $30m received earlier this year from the World Bank towards the cause of science and technology, the Government must by now be thinking of real solutions to intercept the effects of environmental disasters like floods.

Original_Search_Title: Why Corrupt Rulers Hate History, Political Science
Headline: Uganda: Why Corrupt Rulers Hate History, Political Science
Body_Text:
Mr Wafula Oguttu's article, Save UPE Before It Turns a Chronic Disaster, (Daily Monitor on Monday) was extremely depressing reading. Of course, many people foresaw that the Universal Primary Education (UPE) would end up as a gulag of ill-educated children of the poor and working class, but no one would have imagined it would be as bad as Wafula described it.
The scary thing is that even the gloomy picture Wafula paints is not the worst, going by accounts one reads on the Internet about the state of primary school education in the more remote parts of the motherland. I remember almost seven years ago, we were driving on a weekend to a funeral in the western part of Tororo district and took a shortcut off Tirinyi Road.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Learn New Cassava Preservation Method
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Learn New Cassava Preservation Method
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS in Pallisa district have taken to preserving their cassava using a grater. The motor-driven machine can process one metric tonne of cassava per hour, using only 0.8 litres of petrol.
"I used to sell 4kg of cassava at sh800. I feared it would rot. I now get sh1,200 a kilogramme," says George Owere."

Original_Search_Title: Nebbi Inmates Evacuated
Headline: Uganda: Nebbi Inmates Evacuated
Body_Text:
Kampala — Over 180 inmates at Ragem Government Prison in Nebbi district are to be evacuated to a new detention centre after their cells flooded and their toilets were washed away a week ago.
The prison commandant, Simpson Awongo, said the transfer would be carried out after the government had organised all the logistics.

Original_Search_Title: 'No Value Addition Without Money'
Headline: Uganda: 'No Value Addition Without Money'
Body_Text:
Kampala — FRANK Tumwebaze was elected the new president of the Uganda National Farmers Federation (UNFF) in May. He takes over an institution that has made several achievements, but still has serious challenges to handle. Tumwebaze is also MP Kamwenge County. Joshua Kato talked to him about his aspirations:
What inspired you to become the chairman of this group?

Original_Search_Title: Mixing Wheat And Cassava to Reduce Cost of Bread
Headline: Mozambique: Mixing Wheat And Cassava to Reduce Cost of Bread
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Eduardo Mondlane University (UEM), the largest higher education institution in Mozambique, has successfully conducted a study on using a mixture of wheat and cassava flours to produce bread.
The study was ordered by the government, seeking solutions to the repeated increases in the price of wheat flour by the milling companies, which subsequently increases the price of bread.

Original_Search_Title: UN Begins Food Security Drive in North
Headline: Uganda: UN Begins Food Security Drive in North
Body_Text:
Lira — Internally displaced persons and those returning home from the camps in the north may not have to worry about food since the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation has launched a food security drive in the area.
The Fao-funded cassava planting programme that began in November 2006 covers Lango, Acholi and Teso regions.

Original_Search_Title: Zimbabwean Farmers to Build N400m Dairy
Headline: Nigeria: Zimbabwean Farmers to Build N400m Dairy
Body_Text:
Zimbabwean commercial farmers operating in Shonga Edu local government area of Kwara State, have concluded arrangements to establish a N400 million dairy factory.
The commissioner for Agriculture, Prof. Gana Yisa, made this known to newsmen yesterday in Ilorin, stating that the factory would produce about four million litres of milk annually.

Original_Search_Title: White Farmers Plan N400m Dairy Factory
Headline: Nigeria: White Farmers Plan N400m Dairy Factory
Body_Text:
Kwara — Kwara State Commissioner of Agriculture, Professor Gana Yisa, has said Zimbabwean commercial farmers operating in Shonga, Edu Local Government Area of the state, will soon establish a N400-million dairy factory
Yisa said in Ilorin yesterday, that the factory would produce about four million litres of milk annually and would so on be completed, while production will start early 2008.

Original_Search_Title: The Enterprise Sector is Emerging to Cage Unemployment and Sustain Development - Adelaja, DG SME
Headline: Nigeria: The Enterprise Sector is Emerging to Cage Unemployment and Sustain Development - Adelaja, DG SMEDAN
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mrs. Dupe Adelaja, the indefatigable Director-General of the Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria SMEDAN, speaks in this interview held in her office in Abuja, of her determination to grow the enterprise sector to a level it can drive the nation's economy. Excerpts:
HOW can you leverage on the MSMEs national policy recently approved by the Federal Executive Council (FEC) to realise your determination to shape the SMEs sector vibrant enough to sustain economic development?

Original_Search_Title: Unrealised Dreams in the Luwero Triangle
Headline: Uganda: Unrealised Dreams in the Luwero Triangle
Body_Text:
Luweero — At times I ask myself whether this is the government I fought to bring into power. The people up there seem to have forgotten that we once shared a piece of cassava in the bush - former NRA fighter
For Isirayiri Bugembe, 52, a resident of Semuto, Luweero District when the National Resistance Army/Movement (NRA/M) shot its way to power in 1986, it was like reaching the promised land. To him, free education for his eight children, a good job, though he dropped out in primary two, a huge village house and respect from his peers is what lay ahead because of what he says was his role as one of the founding fighters of the NRA under Mr Yoweri Museveni.

Original_Search_Title: Vietnamese Govt Offers to Invest in Ekiti
Headline: Nigeria: Vietnamese Govt Offers to Invest in Ekiti
Body_Text:
Lagos — Efforts of the Engr. Segun Oni led Ekiti State government to attract local and foreign investors to the state have continued to yield results as the Vietnamese Government has joined the league of investors wishing to invest in the state.
Mr. Van Pham Cong, Trade Counselor of Vietnam Trade Office in Nigeria, expressed this position when he visited the governor yesterday. The Counselor said he was in the state to explore possibility of investment collaboration bet-ween Ekiti State and Vietnam.

Original_Search_Title: Global Markets Boost Incomes of Small-Scale Farmers
Headline: Zambia: Global Markets Boost Incomes of Small-Scale Farmers
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Struggling with low prices for their produce in the domestic market, a growing number of small-scale farmers in Zambia have managed to turn their lives around by supplying international markets.
Luke Mbewe, chief executive officer of the Zambia Export Growers Association, an umbrella organisation of small-scale and commercial farmers cultivating vegetables and cut flowers, said returns were high for farmers who targeted the international market, especially the European Union (EU).

Original_Search_Title: Pollution On the Rise
Headline: Uganda: Pollution On the Rise
Body_Text:
Kampala — The increasing number of second hand vehicles in Uganda has seen several informal garages come up to take advantage of mechanical problems associated with used vehicles. These garages are found in almost every corner of the city and its suburbs.
Whereas the garages have created jobs and helped several car owners to get back on the road, poor disposal of waste oil and other effluents has put the health and livelihood of thousands of inhabitants at risk. The executive Director of National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), Dr Aryamanya Mugisha says some wetlands where a variety of staple food crops are illegally grown in the city have been turned into dumping sites for waste products from factories and garages among others.

Original_Search_Title: FG, China, Arab Banks, Others Spend N66 Billion on Food Security
Headline: Nigeria: FG, China, Arab Banks, Others Spend N66 Billion on Food Security
Body_Text:
Lagos — Over N66 BILLION has been spent as a part of a joint effort by the federal government, the Chinese Government and a consortium of international banks in the country to boost food security.
The minister of Agriculture and Water Resources Sayyadi Ruma, gave this disclosure Monday in Abuja, at the opening of the 'inception workshop of NPFS' saying that government's plan to make provision for 'special intervention fund' for the development of the nation's agricultural sector.

Original_Search_Title: Empower Women, Say Goodbye to Poverty
Headline: Nigeria: Empower Women, Say Goodbye to Poverty
Body_Text:
Lagos — The programme organised by Development Support Institute (DSI) in partnership with Esso/Exxon-Mobil at the Banquet Hall, Wellington Hotel, Warri, Delta State, was intended to equip the women in the rural area with basic skills so that they would be empowered to play major roles in the economic arena and contribute more meaningfully in the finance of their immediate family.
At the opening ceremony of the programme, the Executive Director, DSI, Mrs. Jacqueline Odiadi said, "With skills, women can set personal economic goals for themselves, have more respect and feel better fulfilled as human beings in the larger society".

Original_Search_Title: FDC Challenges Were Inevitable From the Start
Headline: Uganda: FDC Challenges Were Inevitable From the Start
Body_Text:
Kampala — SOME analysts have attributed the declining fortunes of the FDC to infiltration by state agents. This analysis is wrong.
FDC was founded on sentiments of a few people whose anger was not stemming from any fundamental disagreements but rather precipitated by factors like dissatisfaction with one's area of deployment, no deployment or dismissal from office.

Original_Search_Title: Bigombe - a Peacemakers' Lonely Battle
Headline: Uganda: Bigombe - a Peacemakers' Lonely Battle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ZACHARY OCHIENG previews the negotiator's upcoming book, 'Turning War into Peace: An Insider's Story'
BETTY BIGOMBE HAS BEEN involved in negotiations to end the Lord's Resistance Army insurgency in northern Uganda since the early 1990s. She has mediated in both public and private capacities, and has worked as a senior social scientist with the World Bank's post-conflict unit. Bigombe, now a senior fellow at the United States Institute of Peace is currently writing a book on the art of mediation. In her project report, she shares her experiences and insights from her work on the conflict in northern Uganda. She discusses the problems posed by intra-state conflicts, her unique role as a female mediator in Africa, and the role of the international community in conflict resolution.

Original_Search_Title: REES Building National Capacity in Cassava Commercialisation
Headline: Zambia: REES Building National Capacity in Cassava Commercialisation
Body_Text:
Ndola — CASSAVA has come to be appreciated in recent times as an effective food security crop and a viable alternative to maize, especially in times of drought.
The crop has been grown by most small-scale farmers, especially in the central and northern parts of the country, for domestic consumption and little was known about its commercial viability.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Force Displaced Back Into Camps
Headline: Uganda: Floods Force IDPs Back Into Camps
Body_Text:
Kampala — THOUSANDS of former internally displaced people (IDPs) in Lango sub-region fled back to the camps over the weekend after their homes were destroyed by floods.
The worst affected areas in Lira are Otuke, Moroto and Erute North counties. Parts of Dokolo, Amolatar and Oyam districts, too, have been hit badly.

Original_Search_Title: 'North and East May Face Food Shortage'
Headline: Uganda: 'North and East May Face Food Shortage'
Body_Text:
Kampala — AGRICULTURE and weather experts have warned that the eastern and northern parts of Uganda, particularly Teso are likely to be hit by food shortage for the next six months.
"There is going to be a disaster because there will be no food for the four districts of Soroti, Amuria, Kumi and Katakwi," said Anuciata Hakuza, an official from the agriculture ministry.

Original_Search_Title: Coffee Producers Are the Biggest Losers
Headline: Uganda: Coffee Producers Are the Biggest Losers
Body_Text:
Kampala — Coffee producers in Uganda suffer from an unfair trade relationship with Europe, even though their beans produce some of the best quality coffee in the world, says the Ugandan coffee industry's governing body.
"The biggest loser is the person directly involved in coffee bean production," says Henry Ngabirano, director of the Uganda Coffee Development Authority. Moreover, Ugandan producers get an "unequal share of the revenue generated by coffee beans".

Original_Search_Title: Floods to Get Worse
Headline: Uganda: Floods to Get Worse
Body_Text:
Kampala — For thousands of people in eastern Uganda, life has become a nightmare as they face Uganda's worst natural disaster so far this year.
Walter Okiring Elakas, a resident of Magoro in Katakwi district, is a sad man indeed.
